title: #eng-ml: 1763429902
preview:
Jonas Weber: dropping the latest telemetry-derived length hists + proposed bins for v1 representative suite. need ML signoff that this is “close enough” to prod shapes (w/o leaking prompt content).

Jonas Weber: doc: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1RedwoodPerfSuiteTelemetry (see `len_hists_us-east_eu-west_2025-11-XX.pdf`)

Logan Wright: quick context: these are from aggregated route-level histograms (no raw prompts). Filtered to top 15 tenants by QPS + sampled across tiers. Excludes internal load tests.

Claire Dubois: proposed input (prefill) token bins:
- A: 0–128
- B: 129–512
- C: 513–2,048
- D: 2,049–8,192
- E: 8,193–32,768

Claire Dubois: output (decode) bins:
- 1–32
- 33–128
- 129–512
- 513+

Claire Dubois: and a first-pass request-mix (by request count):
- chat streaming: 55%
- tool/function calling (multi-turn bursts): 10%
- long-context summarization (big prefill): 15%
- embeddings: 20%

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: thx. first question: are those bins aligned to *model context windows* for our top catalog? (llama/qwen/mistral variants). esp the E bin — will it just be “mostly failures” on some models?

Jonas Weber: good call. for baseline runs we’ll only run E where model context supports it (or cap at model max). but suite definition wants to preserve that tail b/c prod has a real long-context cohort.

Mateo Alvarez: from runtime POV: we’d prefer to keep E, even if some models cap, because it’s exactly where scheduler/backpressure gets spicy. we can make it conditional in the workload config per model.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. harness can validate “requested_ctx <= model_max_ctx” and log when we clip. clipping needs to be explicit in manifest though (so we don’t silently change the workload).

Sana Farid: please don’t do silent clipping. it’ll destroy comparability + makes noise model weird (input lengths drifting). better: define E as “target 16k (or 32k) if supported else skip cohort.”

Logan Wright: re: representativeness — telemetry shows clear elbow at ~2k and ~8k, so C/D split is correct. E is tiny by count (~0.6–1.1% depending on region) but large by token volume.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: ok, but if E is <1% by count, why include it at all in v1? (not arguing, just want rationale spelled out)

Jonas Weber: because it’s disproportionately responsible for tail TTFT incidents + regressions. we want a sentinel workload even if it’s low frequency.

Claire Dubois: + it’s the main knob that stresses KV cache + prefill/decode overlap. perf changes often look fine on A/B/C and then p99 blows up on D/E.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: makes sense. can we make weighting explicit as both “by request” and “by tokens”? product folks keep asking about “what’s most expensive” vs “most common.”

Sana Farid: strongly recommend we report both. for suite composition we can keep the driver as request-count weights, but for analysis we should add token-weighted view.

Rina Kobayashi: harness already exports tokens_in/tokens_out per request. adding a token-weighted aggregation is easy.

Logan Wright: here are the region diffs FYI:
- eu-west has slightly higher median prefill (more enterprise doc summarization)
- us-east has higher chat streaming share
but bins are stable across both.

Jonas Weber: on request mix: the 20% embeddings is from /embeddings route histogram. do we believe that’s fair for “top customer traffic shapes”? (it’s high-QPS but small payload)

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: embeddings share depends heavily on cohort (some customers do tons of retrieval). but we *do* need it in v1 because it exercises different queueing (many small requests) and can cause batching pathologies.

Mateo Alvarez: embeddings is also where we see scheduler fairness issues. + it’s easier to regress “req/s” w/o noticing if we only look at chat.

Claire Dubois: propose we tweak weights slightly:
- chat streaming: 60%
- tool calling: 10%
- long-context: 15%
- embeddings: 15%
Reason: matches overall count share a bit better when excluding a few embedding-heavy outliers.

Logan Wright: i can confirm: if we winsorize tenants at p95 for embeddings-heavy, embeddings share drops from ~20% -> ~14–16% depending on week.

Jonas Weber: ok, that’s a clean story. question for Maya: tool/function calling — do we have enough telemetry to define “bursty multi-turn” realistically, or are we guessing?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: we don’t have perfect visibility into “tools” because it’s app-level, but we can approximate with:
- short prefill (A/B)
- moderate decode (32–128)
- tight inter-arrival in session (client tends to fire next call quickly)
and use a synthetic “session” generator with 3–6 turns + 10–30% turns invoking tools.

Rina Kobayashi: we can implement “sessions” as a workload primitive (N turns, think time distribution). but for perf we need to be careful: think time shouldn’t dominate wall clock.

Sana Farid: yes please keep “think time” small/controlled and include it in manifest. otherwise it’ll add variance and obscure runtime changes.

Claire Dubois: current draft uses think_time_ms ~ Uniform(50, 250). that’s enough to create burstiness without turning it into a soak test.

Mateo Alvarez: fwiw, for scheduler stress we might also want a “microburst” mode: concurrency ramps 50 -> 200 in <10s, then holds. that’s closer to prod launch spikes.

Jonas Weber: yep, but trying to keep v1 minimal. maybe tool-calling workload gets the burst ramp, while chat streaming stays steady-state.

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: on length bins: output bins look fine but i’d add a special case for “very long decode” (1k+ tokens) — do we see that in prod?

Logan Wright: long decode exists but mostly in non-streaming endpoints (batch summarization / report generation). by count it’s small (<2%), but by tokens_out it matters.

Claire Dubois: if we add another bin, suite blows up combinatorially. alternative: keep 513+ as a bin, but for the long-context workload specifically set decode target to 256–768 (not tiny). and maybe add a separate “long decode sentinel” in v2.

Sana Farid: +1. keep v1 stable; add v2 backlog item for “long decode sentinel” once noise model is nailed.

Jonas Weber: sounds like consensus:
1) keep prefill bins A–E, but E is conditional on model context; do not clip silently
2) request-mix weights updated to 60/10/15/15 (chat/tool/longctx/emb)
3) report both request-weighted and token-weighted summaries
4) tool calling approximated via session generator (3–6 turns) with small controlled think time + optional ramp

Jonas Weber: Maya, ok to mark “approved for v1” w/ those edits?

Dr. Maya Srinivasan: approved w/ two asks:
- add a short paragraph in the suite doc explaining the winsorization choice (why embeddings weight isn’t 20%)
- explicitly note that E is a *sentinel* cohort (low count, high tail risk)

Claire Dubois: ack, will update the representative suite doc + add rationale section.

Rina Kobayashi: i’ll add manifest fields: `workload.cohort_id`, `workload.target_ctx_tokens`, `workload.skip_if_unsupported=true`, and ensure validator fails if someone “clips” without stating it.

Mateo Alvarez: thanks all. pls ping me when the session generator PR is up; want to sanity check scheduler knobs for burst mode.

Jonas Weber: will do. logging decision into ADR-0173 notes as well.

Sana Farid: and once we have first runs with these weights, i want to sanity-check variance per cohort before we freeze thresholds.

Logan Wright: i’ll post the updated telemetry snapshot (same filters) so it’s reproducible. will include query hash + time range.

Jonas Weber: perfect. closing loop here.

title: #architecture: 1763335520
preview:
Claire Dubois: Dropping ADR-0172 draft here for review (metrics + run manifest schema). Would love eyes on: schema fields, versioning, and how we encode workload params + env capture without making it impossible to diff / store. Doc: https://confluence.redwoodinference.com/x/ADR-0172-draft

Claire Dubois: Current proposal is 2 JSON artifacts per run:
1) `run_manifest.json` (inputs + env)
2) `results.json` (metrics + aggregations)

Claire Dubois: Biggest open questions called out in ADR:
- Do we version the schema (semver) vs a date stamp?
- Workload params: flatten vs nested (e.g., sequence length bins / distributions)
- Env capture: how strict is “required” (driver/CUDA, clocks, container digest, git SHA, region, etc.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 on splitting manifest vs results. For harness it’s way easier to treat manifest as immutable. But we need to be careful that results references manifest by hash or filename, otherwise dashboards get messy.

Allison Grant: Reading. Two immediate comments:
1) metrics definitions need to be explicit about boundary (server-only vs client-observed). We already have confusion in SLO land.
2) env capture: please include “measurement boundary” fields too, not just driver versions.

Sean Gallagher: For env capture, we should distinguish “must for reproducibility” vs “nice to have”. Otherwise CI is going to fail constantly when a field is missing on some runner.

Noah Patel: On schema versioning: I’d prefer `schema_version: 1` + `schema_revision` semver-ish. Date stamp is ambiguous (and people will cargo-cult it).

Mateo Alvarez: Agree w/ Noah. Also: if we’re going to gate releases on this, we need backwards compatibility story. E.g., runtime adds new scheduler knob → manifests should still validate.

Jonas Weber: For workload params: pls keep them structured, not flattened. If we flatten everything into `workload_param_*` it’ll be unreadable. But we should be consistent about naming and units.

Logan Wright: Dashboarding/data pipeline angle: nested is ok as long as we have a stable path for key fields we group by. E.g. `model.name`, `hardware.gpu_sku`, `region`, `workload.id`, `workload.mode` should be top-level or at least fixed paths.

Connor O'Brien: On CI gating: validator should allow “unknown fields” so we can extend without breaking old runners. But we should lock down required fields.

Sahana Iyer: +1 and please keep the schema compatible with GitHub Actions artifacts size constraints. If env capture includes giant dumps it will blow up. (Put big stuff in separate artifacts and reference by URL/path.)

Claire Dubois: ok, let’s go point by point. Starting w/ versioning: ADR currently proposes
- `manifest_schema_version`: "0.1.0" (semver)
- `results_schema_version`: "0.1.0"
- `workload_suite_version`: "2026q1.v1" (string)
Feedback: are we ok with semver strings? Or do we want integer + changelog?

Noah Patel: Semver strings are fine, but define what increments mean. E.g. major = breaking validation/backwards incompatible, minor = new optional fields, patch = doc/typo.

Allison Grant: +1. And define a migration rule: when we bump major, we need a converter script in repo (even if best-effort).

Rina Kobayashi: From harness: semver string is easiest. We can embed it in filenames too (`run_manifest.v0.1.0.json`) but not required.

Sean Gallagher: Please keep schema versions independent from workload suite versions. Workload suite will change often; schema shouldn’t.

Claire Dubois: Next: workload params. Current sketch:
```
"workload": {
  "id": "chat_streaming",
  "variant": "prod_mix_v1",
  "mode": "streaming",
  "seed": 12345,
  "request_mix": {
    "chat": 0.65,
    "tool": 0.20,
    "summarize": 0.10,
    "other": 0.05
  },
  "distributions": {
    "prompt_tokens": {"p50": 180, "p90": 900, "max": 4096},
    "output_tokens": {"p50": 220, "p90": 800, "max": 2048}
  },
  "concurrency": {"target": 64, "ramp_seconds": 90}
}
```
Question: do we allow arbitrary keys under `distributions`? Or do we standardize on bins/histograms only?

Jonas Weber: For representativeness we often need bins, not just p50/p90/max. p50/p90 hides multimodal mixes. I’d propose we support two forms:
- `histogram_bins`: explicit bin edges + weights
- `summary`: p50/p90/max for quick glance

Logan Wright: From pipeline, histogram bins are fine but please keep the structure consistent. Something like:
`"histogram": {"bins": "0-32,33-64,...", "weights": "0.1,0.2,..."}` is ugly. Better:
```
"prompt_tokens": {
  "type": "histogram",
  "bins": "0-64;65-128;129-256",
  "weights": "0.12;0.34;0.54"
}
```
But I know ADR wants JSON not CSV strings…

Allison Grant: We can keep arrays in JSON but remember our file generation constraints? (Wait—this is internal, arrays are fine in the manifest itself. The Slack-exported file is separate.)

Rina Kobayashi: +1 arrays are fine in the manifest in GitHub. In this thread we’re just agreeing on structure.

Mateo Alvarez: Standardize what matters to perf: prompt token distribution, output token distribution, and arrival process. Everything else optional.

Sean Gallagher: Also need to specify caching scenario. There was a whole thread about cold vs warm. I’d like explicit:
- `cache_mode: cold|warm_prefix|warm_kv` (or similar)
Otherwise results are incomparable.

Connor O'Brien: +1, and if cache_mode impacts gating thresholds it must be a tag in results.

Claire Dubois: Great call. ADR has a footnote but not explicit field. Adding `cache_mode`.

Claire Dubois: Now env capture. Proposed required:
- runtime git SHA + build id
- container image digest
- region + cluster
- GPU sku + count
- driver version
- CUDA version
- runtime flags (scheduler policy id)

Optional:
- GPU clocks locked? (yes/no + value)
- NUMA / CPU pinning
- kernel selection dump path
- tracing artifacts paths

Sean Gallagher: I’d push GPU clocks + power settings into “required” for baseline runs at least. Otherwise variance will be huge and we’ll waste weeks. It can be optional for developer local runs.

Sahana Iyer: How do we encode “required for baseline runs” vs “optional for local”? maybe `run_type: baseline|canary|local` and validator enforces more fields based on run_type.

Allison Grant: Please don’t make validator super complex. But we can do conditional requirements with JSON schema `if/then`. That’s acceptable.

Noah Patel: +1. Keep it simple: `run_intent: baseline` triggers strictness.

Rina Kobayashi: I can implement conditional schema checks in the validator easily (we’re using ajv). We already do this for “streaming requires stream metrics”.

Jonas Weber: Also for env capture: include model config that affects quality/perf: quantization profile, max context, tensor parallel degree, dtype, kv cache dtype, etc.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 and scheduler knobs. But we need a line somewhere; otherwise manifest becomes a dump of every config option. Maybe:
- `config_fingerprint`: hash of full config
- `config_effective`: include only the subset that impacts perf (documented)

Logan Wright: Hash is helpful for de-duping. But dashboards need human-readable fields. So include both hash + a curated set.

Claire Dubois: ok. Drafting: `effective_config` section with curated keys + `full_config_ref` as artifact link.

Allison Grant: Make sure the curated keys include measurement boundary, like:
- `ttft_definition`: `server_first_token_generated` vs `first_byte_on_wire`
- `tpot_definition`: `server_token_interval` (exclude client read)
If we don’t record that, people will “fix” bugs and accidentally change definitions.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. In harness we can set those constants and write them into manifest.

Connor O'Brien: For results: propose we output both raw samples (maybe capped) + aggregated percentiles? Or only percentiles?

Sean Gallagher: For gating, we need percentiles. For triage, raw samples help a lot (to see multimodal). But raw samples get big.

Sahana Iyer: Could do: results has percentiles + a separate `samples.parquet` artifact. Results includes URL. Keep JSON small.

Claire Dubois: Works. ADR already suggests results JSON should be <= ~1MB.

Noah Patel: Another subtlety: multi-model fallback. If the runtime falls back to another kernel/model variant, we should record that. Otherwise baseline might accidentally include fallback path.

Mateo Alvarez: That’s in PR-21519 (kernel selection dump). In manifest we can include `kernel_selection: {"mode": "record", "sample_rate": 0.05}` + results include summary counts.

Rina Kobayashi: I’ll add a results field like `kernel_fallback_rate` and `top_fallback_reasons`. That will help catch silent regressions.

Allison Grant: Careful: “top reasons” is a list. But for JSON results, list is fine. For dashboards we might need it as tags? Might be too high-cardinality. Maybe keep it as a drilldown link.

Logan Wright: +1. High-cardinality reasons will explode metrics. Put detailed reasons in artifact, keep a single scalar in results (e.g., fallback_rate) and a boolean `fallback_occurred`.

Claire Dubois: Summarizing decisions from this thread:
- Versioning: semver strings for manifest/results schema. Define major/minor/patch semantics + provide converter for major bumps.
- Workload params: structured `workload` object; support histograms (bins + weights) AND summary stats; add explicit `cache_mode`.
- Env capture: add `run_intent` to allow conditional required fields; baseline intent requires clock/power settings.
- Config: include curated `effective_config` + `config_fingerprint` + reference to full config artifact.
- Measurement boundary: record TTFT/TPOT definitions in manifest.
- Results: percentiles in JSON; raw samples in separate artifact.

Claire Dubois: I’ll update ADR text + schema sketch accordingly today. If anyone objects, please reply in-thread by EOD tomorrow.

Sean Gallagher: Looks good. Please ping me when you add the baseline strictness part, I want to make sure we capture the exact fields SRE cares about (clocks, placement, isolation).

Allison Grant: +1. Also add a short section in ADR for “metrics tag cardinality guidelines” so people don’t try to put fallback reasons as tags.

Rina Kobayashi: ack. I’ll take an action item to update validator once ADR lands. (PR-21432 will need a follow-up.)

Connor O'Brien: Thanks. Once schema is stable-ish, I’ll wire the version fields into perf-canary gating config so we can refuse unknown majors.

Claire Dubois: Perfect. Closing loop after edits.

title: #eng-infra: 1762643912
preview:
Irene Choi: heads up, for hot-route protection we need to settle which fleet signals are actually reliable for admission control triggers. seeing debate in reviews: GPU saturation vs queue depth vs scheduler lag. want to lock a recommendation this week + note region-specific thresholds.

Vanessa Ortiz: +1. GPU % is the one everyone reaches for but it lies a lot in practice (esp in us-east). queue depth seems closer to “customer pain” but also depends on batching settings.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE view: the main thing is signals that correlate with p95/p99 latency BEFORE we’re on fire. GPU util tends to peg at 95% during healthy steady state for some pools.

Keiko Yamamoto: also autoscaler can mask things. gpu-util can be high while we’re scaling out, so if we shed based on util we’d shed too aggressively.

Jamal Pierce: i pulled a quick snapshot from last week burst (us-east) — GPU util was 92–97% for ~40 mins, but latency only spiked for chat when queue depth crossed a threshold.

Jamal Pierce: excerpt (from notes):
- us-east chat pool baseline gpu_util: ~88–93% at normal daytime
- eu-west baseline gpu_util: ~70–82%
- queue_depth_req (avg) baseline: ~0.2–0.8
- when queue_depth_req sustained > 6, p95 latency started climbing fast

Robert King: so maybe gpu util isn’t a trigger, but still useful as context / to validate we’re not just in a metric bug.

Elena Popov: routing needs something stable and low-cardinality. per-GPU metrics are expensive in decision path; aggregated per-pool / per-cluster is fine. queue depth per route-group would be ideal.

Julian Moore: runtime side: scheduler lag is probably the cleanest leading indicator. We now export `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95` + `runtime.queue_depth_requests` (post PR-27435). Both are already aggregated by pool.

Logan Wright: careful on tags. we had the cardinality incident last month when route_id slipped into overload metrics :grimacing: if routing wants per-route-group, we can do `route_group` not per-tenant.

Aisha Bello: +1. The alerting side also prefers a composite: (queue depth high) AND (sched lag high) for N minutes.

Irene Choi: what about GPU saturation as a guardrail though? e.g. if queue depth low but GPU is pegged, that’s still a sign we’re out of headroom? or could just be long sequences / kernels.

Sofia Ivanova: queue depth low + gpu pegged is often long prompts with continuous batching. scheduler is “busy” but not necessarily behind. it only becomes a problem if lag grows (scheduler can’t keep up) or queue accumulates.

Vanessa Ortiz: also MIG fragmentation can make util look high with less effective throughput. we’ve seen util ~95% even when one slice is starved.

Peter Holtz: for circuit breakers we usually look at:
1) scheduler lag p95 (service time backlog)
2) pending queue depth (arrival > service)
3) 5xx spike (already failing)
GPU util is mostly a “capacity planning” indicator not “admission now” indicator.

Robert King: question: do we have consistent queue depth across regions? i remember eu-west uses a slightly different batching config.

Julian Moore: yes but magnitude differs. queue depth is “requests waiting to be admitted to scheduler” so it depends on front-door concurrency + batch size.

Jamal Pierce: that’s where region-specific thresholds come in. eu-west queue depth baseline is lower; using a global threshold of 6 might be too high (we’d react late). maybe use percentile over baseline per region/pool.

Aisha Bello: +100. absolute thresholds are brittle. can we do: trigger when `queue_depth` > max(absolute_min, baseline_p95 * factor)?

Keiko Yamamoto: baseline computation in routing sounds risky for data plane complexity. but we can precompute per-region thresholds in config (control plane) and ship them.

Elena Popov: yep, we can keep routing simple:
- read per-route-group budget
- read per-region “pressure thresholds” for pool
- evaluate with last N seconds aggregated metrics from fleet signals service
No dynamic baseline calc in routing.

Irene Choi: can infra own the per-region threshold table (at least initial values) and we can revisit after canary?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. propose:
Signal A (primary): scheduler_lag_ms_p95
Signal B (secondary): queue_depth_requests_avg (or p95)
Signal C (context only): gpu_util_p95

Vanessa Ortiz: then define pressure states per region/pool:
- WARN: lag > X OR qdepth > Y for 2m
- HOT: lag > X2 AND qdepth > Y2 for 1m
- CRIT: lag > X3 AND qdepth > Y3 for 30s (activate strict admission)

Rafael Mendes: this maps nicely to “start shedding best-effort at HOT, protect enterprise budgets at CRIT”.

Logan Wright: for dashboards, please standardize names:
- `fleet.pressure_state{region,pool}` as a gauge (0/1/2/3)
- `overload.shed_rate{region,route_group,tier,reason}`
We can annotate when pressure_state flips.

Peter Holtz: note: pressure_state should be derived in control plane / fleet signals service, not computed differently by each component.

Robert King: do we have an agreed metric source? i’ve seen both prometheus and the fleet-signals gRPC.

Vanessa Ortiz: for this project: fleet-signals gRPC (aggregated) is the source of truth. prom is for debugging.

Jamal Pierce: region notes (tentative, based on last 14d):
- us-east (chat pool): WARN lag>120ms or qdepth>4; HOT lag>250ms + qdepth>8; CRIT lag>400ms + qdepth>12
- eu-west (chat pool): WARN lag>90ms or qdepth>3; HOT lag>200ms + qdepth>6; CRIT lag>320ms + qdepth>9
These need validation during game day.

Aisha Bello: add a “don’t flap” rule please. if we enter HOT/CRIT require 60s below WARN to exit.

Keiko Yamamoto: also check autoscaling interaction: when scale-out in progress, lag can oscillate. maybe incorporate `pending_scale_actions` as a dampener? (not a trigger)

Sofia Ivanova: be careful: when scale-out happens, new replicas warm KV cache etc. you still might want to shed briefly to protect tail.

Irene Choi: ok, decision direction:
- use scheduler lag + queue depth for triggers
- GPU util is contextual only
- thresholds per region/pool in config, owned by infra + tuned via canary/game day

Irene Choi: action items:
1) @Jamal Pierce share the threshold table draft in the rollout doc + include “how derived”
2) @Vanessa Ortiz + @Robert King confirm fleet-signals gRPC fields + update contract doc
3) @Elena Popov ensure routing consumes pressure_state + doesn’t query raw gpu util
4) @Logan Wright align dashboard queries + make sure tags are bounded (region/pool/route_group only)

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll post the gRPC fields here once i confirm final names.

Julian Moore: current names from runtime exporter (double-check):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95{region,pool}`
- `runtime.queue_depth_requests_avg{region,pool}`
- `runtime.gpu_utilization_p95{region,pool}`
Fleet-signals service already aggregates to `fleet.*` equivalents.

Rafael Mendes: once we have the table, we’ll run it in the game day and see if we start shedding at the right time (before enterprise p95 blows past SLO). :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: cool. please keep this thread updated with any “gotchas” discovered in eu-west; we’ve been burned by copying us-east thresholds before.

2) pending queue depth (arrival > service)
3) 5xx spike (already failing)
GPU util is mostly a “capacity planning” indicator not “admission now” indicator.

Robert King: question: do we have consistent queue depth across regions? i remember eu-west uses a slightly different batching config.

Julian Moore: yes but magnitude differs. queue depth is “requests waiting to be admitted to scheduler” so it depends on front-door concurrency + batch size.

Jamal Pierce: that’s where region-specific thresholds come in. eu-west queue depth baseline is lower; using a global threshold of 6 might be too high (we’d react late). maybe use percentile over baseline per region/pool.

Aisha Bello: +100. absolute thresholds are brittle. can we do: trigger when `queue_depth` > max(absolute_min, baseline_p95 * factor)?

Keiko Yamamoto: baseline computation in routing sounds risky for data plane complexity. but we can precompute per-region thresholds in config (control plane) and ship them.

Elena Popov: yep, we can keep routing simple:
- read per-route-group budget
- read per-region “pressure thresholds” for pool
- evaluate with last N seconds aggregated metrics from fleet signals service
No dynamic baseline calc in routing.

Irene Choi: can infra own the per-region threshold table (at least initial values) and we can revisit after canary?

Vanessa Ortiz: yes. propose:
Signal A (primary): scheduler_lag_ms_p95
Signal B (secondary): queue_depth_requests_avg (or p95)
Signal C (context only): gpu_util_p95

Vanessa Ortiz: then define pressure states per region/pool:
- WARN: lag > X OR qdepth > Y for 2m
- HOT: lag > X2 AND qdepth > Y2 for 1m
- CRIT: lag > X3 AND qdepth > Y3 for 30s (activate strict admission)

Rafael Mendes: this maps nicely to “start shedding best-effort at HOT, protect enterprise budgets at CRIT”.

Logan Wright: for dashboards, please standardize names:
- `fleet.pressure_state{region,pool}` as a gauge (0/1/2/3)
- `overload.shed_rate{region,route_group,tier,reason}`
We can annotate when pressure_state flips.

Peter Holtz: note: pressure_state should be derived in control plane / fleet signals service, not computed differently by each component.

Robert King: do we have an agreed metric source? i’ve seen both prometheus and the fleet-signals gRPC.

Vanessa Ortiz: for this project: fleet-signals gRPC (aggregated) is the source of truth. prom is for debugging.

Jamal Pierce: region notes (tentative, based on last 14d):
- us-east (chat pool): WARN lag>120ms or qdepth>4; HOT lag>250ms + qdepth>8; CRIT lag>400ms + qdepth>12
- eu-west (chat pool): WARN lag>90ms or qdepth>3; HOT lag>200ms + qdepth>6; CRIT lag>320ms + qdepth>9
These need validation during game day.

Aisha Bello: add a “don’t flap” rule please. if we enter HOT/CRIT require 60s below WARN to exit.

Keiko Yamamoto: also check autoscaling interaction: when scale-out in progress, lag can oscillate. maybe incorporate `pending_scale_actions` as a dampener? (not a trigger)

Sofia Ivanova: be careful: when scale-out happens, new replicas warm KV cache etc. you still might want to shed briefly to protect tail.

Irene Choi: ok, decision direction:
- use scheduler lag + queue depth for triggers
- GPU util is contextual only
- thresholds per region/pool in config, owned by infra + tuned via canary/game day

Irene Choi: action items:
1) @Jamal Pierce share the threshold table draft in the rollout doc + include “how derived”
2) @Vanessa Ortiz + @Robert King confirm fleet-signals gRPC fields + update contract doc
3) @Elena Popov ensure routing consumes pressure_state + doesn’t query raw gpu util
4) @Logan Wright align dashboard queries + make sure tags are bounded (region/pool/route_group only)

Vanessa Ortiz: ack. i’ll post the gRPC fields here once i confirm final names.

Julian Moore: current names from runtime exporter (double-check):
- `runtime.scheduler_lag_ms_p95{region,pool}`
- `runtime.queue_depth_requests_avg{region,pool}`
- `runtime.gpu_utilization_p95{region,pool}`
Fleet-signals service already aggregates to `fleet.*` equivalents.

Rafael Mendes: once we have the table, we’ll run it in the game day and see if we start shedding at the right time (before enterprise p95 blows past SLO). :thumbsup:

Irene Choi: cool. please keep this thread updated with any “gotchas” discovered in eu-west; we’ve been burned by copying us-east thresholds before.

title: #architecture: 1759953233
preview:
Rafael Mendes: pulling in #architecture folks for a decision we keep circling. where does the source-of-truth for the public status page component registry live? (component ids, display names, owner, mapping rules, rollout flags). options: repo config vs Confluence page.

Christine Wong: +1. current pain is drift: confluence has “proposed” list, incident-bot has a partial copy, dashboards use slightly diff names.

Irene Choi: repo wins by default for “source of truth” if it’s consumed by tooling. confluence should be a rendered view, not authoritative.

Allison Grant: agree in principle. from observability side: we need the registry to drive dashboard generation + alert labels. we can validate in CI if it’s in git.

Miles Thornton: if it’s in Confluence, the only “validation” is social. also harder to roll back a bad change mid-incident.

Rafael Mendes: concerns I’ve heard:
- oncall needs an easy way to see/edit component list
- product/docs want a human-readable canonical description
- security/legal wants review for anything that changes customer-facing language

Ethan Park: oncall editing in Confluence sounds nice until it breaks automation. we should not be editing customer-facing components during an incident anyway (other than status updates). component taxonomy changes should be planned.

Dev Patel: from Support POV, the main thing is: if the public labels change, support macros and customer comms need to match. having it in git is fine as long as there’s an approval step / heads up.

Logan Wright: if we put it in repo, we can generate the confluence page from it (or at least link it). but the source should be machine-readable.

Christine Wong: what repo? incident-bot repo? observability-pack? main redwood mono?

Allison Grant: prefer a neutral/shared place. observability-pack already has dashboard config + the schema work in flight. also ties to the “signals driving status” standard.

Devon Price: incident-bot just consumes it. i’d rather not host the registry there.

Mina Haddad: needs to be accessible to routing/region taxonomy too. if it’s in observability-pack, is that okay for runtime/platform folks?

Irene Choi: yes if we do proper CODEOWNERS and reviews. we already have cross-team config in repos.

Rafael Mendes: proposal:
1) source-of-truth registry in git (observability-pack) as `statuspage/component-registry.json`
2) JSON schema + CI validation (ids stable, required fields present, no dup display names per region group, etc)
3) Confluence page becomes “published view” that mirrors the registry + additional narrative, but explicitly NOT canonical

Miles Thornton: +1 and add: keep a changelog section in the file header or a `CHANGELOG.md` so we can answer “when did this component split happen?”

Elliot Price: docs angle: if the definitions are in JSON, we can still have docs that explain “what does Hosted API – US/East mean” and link to the registry. but i don’t want docs to be blocked by code review every time we tweak wording.

Michael Grant: legal concern is not the location, it’s control. if a change modifies customer-facing language (display names, descriptions), we need an approval gate. otherwise we risk inconsistent promises.

Naomi Feldman: security/compliance: +1 to auditability. git gives an audit trail. Confluence edits are harder to lock down and review consistently.

Christine Wong: so maybe separate “immutable ids + owner + mapping” vs “long-form description”? the description field could be optional / lightweight.

Allison Grant: i’d still keep a short description in registry for UI/tooling, but not a marketing blurb. anything more verbose lives in docs/confluence.

Dev Patel: review requirement suggestion:
- any change that adds/removes a component OR changes public display_name requires SRE + Support signoff
- changes to ownership/contact can be lighter weight

Irene Choi: also need infra/platform signoff when mapping rules change (service->component). it can impact paging + dashboards.

Rafael Mendes: ok, let’s be concrete on review rules.
Draft:
- CODEOWNERS for `/statuspage/component-registry.json` includes `@eng-sre` + `@eng-platform` + `@observability`
- if `public_display_name` or `public_grouping` changes: require `@support` reviewer + ping `#support`
- if `mapping_rules` changes: require `@eng-platform` reviewer (and `@incident-bot` as FYI)
- if `description` changes only: regular CODEOWNERS ok, but keep it factual

Miles Thornton: add a “rollout flag” in registry (e.g., `enabled: false` until dashboards + incident-bot are ready). prevents partial launch.

Devon Price: +1. incident-bot can read only enabled components.

Logan Wright: CI checks could include “no enabled component without dashboard link” to avoid half-wired entries.

Christine Wong: how do we handle Confluence? i can update the existing taxonomy page to say “generated from registry” and link to the file + ADR.

Elliot Price: i can work with Wei to adjust wording in templates to refer to component ids consistently. but please don’t make Confluence the place people go to copy/paste names.

Naomi Feldman: request: document who can approve emergency changes and how to revert. i’d rather we never do it, but reality.

Rafael Mendes: emergency changes: treat as normal PR with expedited reviewers. no direct pushes. if Sev0 and we absolutely must correct a mislabel, IC can request fast-track review from oncall SRE + one of (Infra/Obs). but ideally we only change status updates, not taxonomy.

Michael Grant: that’s acceptable. add note: any change that alters customer-facing wording during an active incident needs comms lead awareness.

Allison Grant: we should capture all of this in an ADR so it’s not “Slack law.”

Rafael Mendes: yep, i’ll write ADR-014 today with:
- decision: source-of-truth in git (observability-pack)
- confluence is informational mirror
- review requirements + CODEOWNERS
- rollout + validation strategy

Christine Wong: can we also explicitly state: component IDs never change once enabled? only add new + deprecate old.

Irene Choi: +1. stable ids are key for metrics continuity.

Dev Patel: and deprecation should have a window (e.g., 30 days) so support can update macros.

Miles Thornton: (reactions: :thumbsup: x4)

Rafael Mendes: sounds like consensus: repo as source-of-truth, Confluence as mirror. i’ll post ADR draft in this thread for final comments.

Allison Grant: works. once ADR lands, we can unblock the schema PR + mapping work.

Devon Price: +1 please tag me on ADR. i’ll align incident-bot consumption to new path.

Christine Wong: i’ll update the taxonomy page with a banner once ADR is approved.

Rafael Mendes: closing the loop: target to finalize ADR by EOD tomorrow so ENG-4821 can proceed without ambiguity.

title: #eng: 1759942015
preview:
Jae Kim: quick q on the status page component mapping work — for route/capability tagging, what’s the expected labels/values? i’m looking at gateway metrics + a couple workers and want to make sure we tag in the “approved” way so it actually maps to public components.

Logan Wright: yup, we standardized on 3 dims for anything that can drive status/public summaries:
- `component_id`
- `region`
- `capability`
This is the same set incident-bot is collecting in intake now.

Jae Kim: ok but what counts as “capability” vs “route”? e.g. `/v1/chat/completions` + stream=true is that “chat” or “streaming”? + do we include model family anywhere?

Mina Haddad: capability is intentionally coarse + user-visible. don’t put model names in tags.
Rules of thumb:
- `/v1/chat/completions` (non-stream) => `capability=chat`
- `/v1/chat/completions` with `stream=true` OR SSE endpoint => `capability=streaming`
- `/v1/embeddings` => `capability=embeddings`
- `/v1/rerank` => `capability=reranking`
If something is “internal route-ish” (scheduler calls, prefill service), keep that as internal labels but still map outward to a capability.

Christine Wong: +1. also: `component_id` is product surface, not service. for almost all request-path metrics in prod it should be `component_id=hosted_api` (Dedicated/Private are different; we can go over those separately).

Allison Grant: docs pointer: Confluence has the taxonomy + definitions: “Status page component taxonomy” (SRE → status-page-and-comms). the canonical ids are lowercase snake_case. pls don’t invent new ones ad-hoc.

Jae Kim: do we have a schema somewhere i can validate against? i keep fat-fingering region names.

Logan Wright: yep, schema is in the registry validation PR. the “single source of truth” is a JSON registry file (and a JSON schema that CI validates). you can search in the observability-pack repo for “status component registry schema”.

Caleb Johnson: regions should match what routing uses today (no cloud provider AZs). examples we’re using:
- `us-east`
- `us-west`
- `eu-west`
- `ap-southeast`
If you only know “global” (rare), we’d rather you plumb region through than tag `global`.

Devon Price: for incident-bot intake fields we also use those exact strings. so if you tag metrics with `region=us-east` and pick `capability=streaming`, the bot drafts will line up with the component options.

Jae Kim: can someone drop an example tag block for gateway?

Christine Wong: from the gateway side (pseudo):
```python
metrics.increment(
  "gateway.request",
  tags={
    "component_id": "hosted_api",
    "region": request.region,  # e.g. "us-east"
    "capability": capability_from_request(request),
  },
)
```
`capability_from_request` should be based on endpoint + stream flag. not on model.

Mina Haddad: and if you’re doing this in traces too, same keys. we want to be able to pivot dashboards + summaries by the same dims.

Allison Grant: re: “route” — if you really need it for debugging, keep it as `route_name` or `endpoint` internally, but do not use it as a public-facing grouping. we had the “API ok but embeddings failing” issue because things were tagged as generic API without the capability split.

Jae Kim: makes sense. what about dedicated control plane? i’m touching a metric in the capacity scheduler.

Rafael Mendes: dedicated control plane is a separate component_id (not `hosted_api`). the taxonomy doc calls it out as “Dedicated: Control Plane” and we’re exposing a limited set of signals publicly. if your metric affects customer-facing provisioning/scale actions, tag it to that. if it’s purely internal noise, don’t.

Devon Price: + note: dedicated/private incidents have comms constraints. don’t include customer identifiers in tags/labels. component_id + region + capability is safe.

Elliot Price: if you need “what do we say publicly” guidance, docs side has a short section under incident templates. but for tagging, stick to the schema + examples above.

Jae Kim: awesome. i’ll implement with `capability=streaming` on chat stream path + keep `route_name` internal. will ping if CI yells at me.

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: if CI fails, it’ll usually point at invalid enum value. easiest fix is copy/paste from the registry.

Logan Wright: also worth checking the hotfix PR we landed last week (misclassified embeddings vs chat). the failure mode was exactly “wrong capability tag → wrong component update”. so thank you for being careful here 🙏

title: #eng-sre: 1759882210
preview:
Rafael Mendes: Kicking off a thread on status page component taxonomy. We need to stop calling everything “API” :grimacing:. Proposal: split Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings (and maybe Reranking) + split by region. Want feedback on shape + naming.

Christine Wong: +1. Current single “API” component is killing us in comms. Customers see “Operational” while embeddings is on fire.

Mina Haddad: For region split, do we want cloud region names (us-east-1/us-west-2/eu-west-1) or friendly (US East/US West/EU West)? Friendly is better for customers, but mapping has to be unambiguous.

Allison Grant: Observability side: we can tag metrics by `region` already; the gateway has `pop` and `cluster` labels. Friendly display name is fine as long as component_id uses canonical region key.

Aisha Bello: If we do product x region, number of components blows up quickly. What’s our target component count? Statuspage UI gets unwieldy >~25 components.

Rafael Mendes: Right now we’re thinking: only split regions for customer-facing request paths (Hosted API + Streaming + Embeddings + Reranking). Console stays global. Dedicated/Private handled separately/limited exposure.

Miles Thornton: +1 to keeping it mostly a 2D grid. Also recommend we treat Streaming as capability, not just “Chat”. It’s a distinct failure mode (disconnects, stalled tokens) even when non-streaming completions look fine.

Christine Wong: Draft list (WIP):
- Hosted API (US East)
- Hosted API (US West)
- Hosted API (EU West)
- Streaming (US East)
- Streaming (US West)
- Streaming (EU West)
- Embeddings (US East)
- Embeddings (US West)
- Embeddings (EU West)
- Reranking (US East)
- Reranking (US West)
- Reranking (EU West)
- Console (Global)

Mina Haddad: That’s 13 components. That feels manageable.

Aisha Bello: For Hosted API, are we mixing chat + text generation? If a model router issue breaks only chat routes, we’ll still over-report.

Rafael Mendes: Good q. My bias: keep Hosted API as “non-streaming text generation/chat request acceptance” and call out route-level details in incident updates. We can add a “Chat” component later if needed, but I’d rather not go to 20+ right away.

Allison Grant: We *can* separate chat vs completions based on route tags (`/v1/chat/completions` vs `/v1/completions`). But we’d need consistency across SDKs + gateway normalization.

Logan Wright: Gateway emits `route_name` today but it’s messy (some are templated, some are raw paths). If we make “Chat” a public component, we need clean cardinality + a mapping table.

Irene Choi: Infra perspective: splitting chat vs text gen might create more confusion unless we have super crisp definitions. Customers think “chat” = their product, not our endpoint.

Dev Patel: Support POV: the #1 complaint is “embeddings failing but status page green.” #2 is “streaming disconnects but you say API operational.” I haven’t seen many tickets asking for chat vs completions split.

Sean Gallagher: Agree w/ Dev. Let’s fix the high-signal pain first: embeddings + streaming as first-class components, split by region. We can iterate once we have accuracy audits.

Aisha Bello: For region grouping, can we align to the 3 regions customers commonly use: US East, US West, EU? What about APAC?

Mina Haddad: APAC usage exists but it’s mostly via partner/edge right now; traffic often routes to US West anyway. We can add APAC once we have a distinct region/control plane.

Allison Grant: From dashboards side, we currently have clean partitions for `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`. APAC would be “other” today. I’d rather not publish “Other”.

Christine Wong: Naming: “Hosted API” vs “API Requests” vs “Inference API”? Trying to avoid ambiguity w/ Dedicated.

Rafael Mendes: Proposal:
- “Hosted API” for standard request/response
- “Hosted Streaming” for SSE streaming
- “Hosted Embeddings”
- “Hosted Reranking”
Then in incident templates we’ll say “Hosted API (US East) experiencing elevated 5xx”.

Dev Patel: Please keep “Hosted” in the name. Otherwise enterprise customers think Dedicated is included in the same component.

Miles Thornton: +1, and component_ids should include deployment mode:
`hosted_api_us_east`, `hosted_streaming_us_east`, `hosted_embeddings_us_east`, etc.
Display names: “Hosted API (US East)”.

Logan Wright: Mapping rule suggestion (so we don’t fight this during incidents):
- If request has `capability=embeddings` => embeddings component
- Else if `streaming=true` => streaming component
- Else => hosted_api
Edge case: rerank is non-streaming but not “api” (customers care).

Allison Grant: Also need clear region selection rule:
- Use serving cluster region (where inference happened), not where customer originated.
Otherwise status can show US East impacted even if we failed over to US West.

Mina Haddad: Agree. Region should represent where we’re serving from / where capacity is impacted.

Aisha Bello: How do we handle partial failures (e.g., only some models)? We won’t have per-model components.

Sean Gallagher: That’s OK. Component degraded + incident text should specify “some models” / “subset of traffic” without listing customer names or capacity details.

Christine Wong: I’ll add definition blurbs:
- Hosted API: non-streaming generation + request acceptance
- Hosted Streaming: streaming responses (SSE) delivery and token streaming
- Hosted Embeddings: embedding generation
- Hosted Reranking: rerank endpoint

Dev Patel: Also add “what it does NOT include” for each (e.g., Console, Dedicated). Helps support macros.

Rafael Mendes: Action items:
1) Christine to turn this into canonical taxonomy doc + component definitions.
2) Mina/Logan to propose region + mapping rules (cluster region).
3) Allison/Aisha to sanity check dashboards/alerts exist per component.
Goal: ready for PR on component registry this week.

Allison Grant: I’ll confirm the metric queries. We should include both error rate + latency p95 for “degraded” criteria, and hysteresis to avoid flapping.

Miles Thornton: Also think about grouping on Statuspage UI: group by product (Hosted API/Streaming/Embeddings/Reranking) then by region underneath.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: Let’s proceed with the 13-component list. If someone feels strongly about adding “Chat” as a separate component, propose it with ticket + evidence.

Christine Wong: Cool, I’ll draft the doc and post here for review EOD. (Will include the component_id naming + ownership placeholders.)
text:
Rafael Mendes: Kicking off a thread on status page component taxonomy. We need to stop calling everything “API” :grimacing:. Proposal: split Hosted API vs Streaming vs Embeddings (and maybe Reranking) + split by region. Want feedback on shape + naming.

Christine Wong: +1. Current single “API” component is killing us in comms. Customers see “Operational” while embeddings is on fire.

Mina Haddad: For region split, do we want cloud region names (us-east-1/us-west-2/eu-west-1) or friendly (US East/US West/EU West)? Friendly is better for customers, but mapping has to be unambiguous.

Allison Grant: Observability side: we can tag metrics by `region` already; the gateway has `pop` and `cluster` labels. Friendly display name is fine as long as component_id uses canonical region key.

Aisha Bello: If we do product x region, number of components blows up quickly. What’s our target component count? Statuspage UI gets unwieldy >~25 components.

Rafael Mendes: Right now we’re thinking: only split regions for customer-facing request paths (Hosted API + Streaming + Embeddings + Reranking). Console stays global. Dedicated/Private handled separately/limited exposure.

Miles Thornton: +1 to keeping it mostly a 2D grid. Also recommend we treat Streaming as capability, not just “Chat”. It’s a distinct failure mode (disconnects, stalled tokens) even when non-streaming completions look fine.

Christine Wong: Draft list (WIP):
- Hosted API (US East)
- Hosted API (US West)
- Hosted API (EU West)
- Streaming (US East)
- Streaming (US West)
- Streaming (EU West)
- Embeddings (US East)
- Embeddings (US West)
- Embeddings (EU West)
- Reranking (US East)
- Reranking (US West)
- Reranking (EU West)
- Console (Global)

Mina Haddad: That’s 13 components. That feels manageable.

Aisha Bello: For Hosted API, are we mixing chat + text generation? If a model router issue breaks only chat routes, we’ll still over-report.

Rafael Mendes: Good q. My bias: keep Hosted API as “non-streaming text generation/chat request acceptance” and call out route-level details in incident updates. We can add a “Chat” component later if needed, but I’d rather not go to 20+ right away.

Allison Grant: We *can* separate chat vs completions based on route tags (`/v1/chat/completions` vs `/v1/completions`). But we’d need consistency across SDKs + gateway normalization.

Logan Wright: Gateway emits `route_name` today but it’s messy (some are templated, some are raw paths). If we make “Chat” a public component, we need clean cardinality + a mapping table.

Irene Choi: Infra perspective: splitting chat vs text gen might create more confusion unless we have super crisp definitions. Customers think “chat” = their product, not our endpoint.

Dev Patel: Support POV: the #1 complaint is “embeddings failing but status page green.” #2 is “streaming disconnects but you say API operational.” I haven’t seen many tickets asking for chat vs completions split.

- “Hosted Streaming” for SSE streaming

- “Hosted Reranking”
Then in incident templates we’ll say “Hosted API (US East) experiencing elevated 5xx”.

Dev Patel: Please keep “Hosted” in the name. Otherwise enterprise customers think Dedicated is included in the same component.

Miles Thornton: +1, and component_ids should include deployment mode:
`hosted_api_us_east`, `hosted_streaming_us_east`, `hosted_embeddings_us_east`, etc.
Display names: “Hosted API (US East)”.

Logan Wright: Mapping rule suggestion (so we don’t fight this during incidents):
- If request has `capability=embeddings` => embeddings component
- Else if `streaming=true` => streaming component
- Else => hosted_api

- Use serving cluster region (where inference happened), not where customer originated.
Otherwise status can show US East impacted even if we failed over to US West.

Mina Haddad: Agree. Region should represent where we’re serving from / where capacity is impacted.

Aisha Bello: How do we handle partial failures (e.g., only some models)? We won’t have per-model components.

Sean Gallagher: That’s OK. Component degraded + incident text should specify “some models” / “subset of traffic” without listing customer names or capacity details.

Christine Wong: I’ll add definition blurbs:

- Hosted Streaming: streaming responses (SSE) delivery and token streaming
- Hosted Embeddings: embedding generation
- Hosted Reranking: rerank endpoint

title: #eng-oncall: 1749564412
preview:
Sean Gallagher: seeing a short spike in 429s starting ~2 mins ago. looks tied to benchmark launch traffic. anyone else seeing this?

Incident Bot: *Alert* `bench-results-service` elevated 429 rate
- window: last 5m
- 429 ratio: 3.8% (baseline <0.2%)
- top path: `/v1/benchmarks/results?format=json`
- region: us-east (edge)
- dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/bench-results

Neha Kapoor: yup, I’m on the same dashboard. p95 looks ok-ish but 429s are real. is this our edge rate limiter or app-level?

Sean Gallagher: looks like edge token bucket. rate limit header present, `x-redwood-ratelimit-policy=public-results-anon`.

Felix Schneider: fwiw the interactive table hits results endpoint a lot on first load + when people flip filters quickly. we also have some prefetch for “top models” that might be too aggressive.

Logan Wright: analytics shows a ton of repeated loads from same IP blocks (likely scrapers + people refreshing). top ASNs are a couple cloud providers.

Omar El-Sayed: quick check in logs: app is returning 200s fine. 429s are before it hits service (no request IDs in app logs). so this is definitely edge/WAF policy.

Vanessa Ortiz: any chance we’re saturating capacity and edge is “protecting” downstream? or is it a hard limit unrelated to fleet?

Sean Gallagher: hard limit. currently 60 req/min per IP for anon on that path. during the spike we’re seeing ~200-400 req/min from a handful of IPs.

Neha Kapoor: we should be careful about loosening globally and getting scraped to death. can we split policy: higher limit for CDN-cached artifact path + keep strict for uncached query endpoint?

Felix Schneider: currently the page requests `/v1/benchmarks/results?format=json` which is dynamic (though effectively versioned by `release=2025-07-10`). we could switch to fetching the versioned artifact URL and let CDN handle it.

Alicia Perez: from web side: we can ship a quick patch to use the versioned JSON asset if that helps. but that’s not immediate.

Harish Reddy: if we raise limit for an hour, any risk to other prod traffic? launch is hot right now.

Sean Gallagher: risk is mostly increased load on the results service + cache layer. but right now 429 is blocking legit users too. we can do a scoped bump (public-results-anon only) and tighten back after peak.

Neha Kapoor: proposal: bump anon limit from 60 -> 180 req/min per IP for 2 hours, AND add a second rule blocking obvious scraper behavior (e.g., >X distinct query params per minute). do we have that capability fast?

Sean Gallagher: fast path is just bumping the token bucket. smarter rule needs config review (and might false positive). I’d rather bump now + monitor origin RPS + error rate.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. origin can handle more. current origin RPS is ~35, would maybe go to 80-100. still fine.

Logan Wright: also: a lot of the requests are identical. cache hit ratio at edge for that endpoint is low because of query params ordering (some callers send `format=json&release=...` vs `release=...&format=json`). might be hurting us.

Felix Schneider: oof yeah. we can normalize query param order on client, but not today. if edge caching key is strict, we’re missing cache.

Sean Gallagher: ok I’m going to bump `public-results-anon` to 180 rpm and add a note in #incidents thread. if origin starts to sweat, we revert.

Incident Bot: *Change applied* `edge-rate-limit/public-results-anon`
- previous: 60 req/min/IP
- new: 180 req/min/IP
- requested by: Sean Gallagher

Neha Kapoor: watching graphs. 429s dropping already.

Logan Wright: 429 ratio now 0.6% and falling. CTA clicks are up again, so user impact likely resolved.

Vanessa Ortiz: any need for extra fleet capacity as a result of this? (I can poke Harish if we need reserves.)

Sean Gallagher: not yet. keep an eye on origin CPU + cache. if we cross 70% sustained we’ll consider scaling.

Felix Schneider: action item for tomorrow: move interactive page to fetch versioned artifact (static URL) + reduce prefetch. I’ll open a ticket and tag you all.

Neha Kapoor: also add `Retry-After` header on 429 if we’re not already—helps clients behave.

Omar El-Sayed: edge is already adding `retry-after: 1` (saw it in curl). good there.

Sean Gallagher: cool. leaving the bumped limit in place until 2 hrs post-peak unless we see abuse. please ping if 429s creep back.

Neha Kapoor: :thumbsup:

Harish Reddy: :eyes: monitoring. ping me if we need to scale cache/origin.

Alicia Perez: thanks all — if you want us to add a banner/tooltip about “don’t refresh like crazy” we can, but hopefully unnecessary.

Sean Gallagher: lol. let’s avoid that. better to fix the fetch/caching path.

Neha Kapoor: agreed. closing loop: short-term mitigation done. long-term fix is static artifact fetch + cache key normalization + prefetch tuning.

title: #eng: 1747333303
preview:
Priya Natarajan: We’re close to flipping the oncall handoff for the PLG signal router. Can folks drop the *must-have* metrics + alert conditions? Thinking HubSpot 429s, retries, dropped events, lag, etc. Also owners + dashboard link placeholders so we can wire this into PagerDuty.

Logan Wright: +1. From telemetry/analytics side, the top things that will bite us:
- event lag (telemetry -> analytics table -> scoring output)
- aggregator job failures / partial writes
- dropped events / DLQ growth
If we don’t alert on lag, sales will just see “nothing happening” and assume routing is broken.

Mei Lin: For the daily aggregation job (ENG-7732 / PR-28458), we should alert on:
- `aggregation_job_success` (1/0) per run
- `aggregation_rows_written` (unexpectedly low vs trailing 7d baseline)
- `aggregation_backfill_runs` (if backfill is running, suppress some lag alerts)
Also a gauge for `analytics_table_partition_freshness_hours`.

Caleb Johnson: Ingestion/data quality:
- `telemetry_event_ingest_lag_seconds` p95 and max
- `telemetry_events_dropped_total` (with reason label: schema_invalid, auth, throttled)
- `telemetry_events_dlq_depth`
We should page only on sustained DLQ growth + lag, not on occasional schema_invalid spikes (docs instrumentation can be noisy).

Ethan Park: HubSpot integration metrics (PR-28503 + PR-28544):
- `hubspot_requests_total` by endpoint
- `hubspot_429s_total` + `hubspot_rate_limited_seconds_total`
- `hubspot_5xx_total`
- `hubspot_retries_total` + `hubspot_retry_queue_depth`
- `hubspot_task_create_failures_total`
- `hubspot_company_upsert_failures_total`
Also *idempotency* issues are subtle — a counter for `hubspot_deduped_updates_total` would help validate we’re not spamming timeline events.

Nadia Rahman: Scoring service should expose:
- `signals_created_total` (by signal_type)
- `signals_suppressed_total` (by suppression_reason)
- `signals_routed_total` (by destination=hubspot)
- `signals_failed_total` (validation_failed, missing_company_mapping, hubspot_error)
And a histogram: `signal_end_to_end_latency_seconds` (telemetry timestamp -> task created)

Marcus Lin: From security side: please add a metric + alert if we ever attempt to send disallowed fields to HubSpot.
Like `hubspot_payload_policy_violations_total` (should be 0 always). If >0, page the oncall + auto-disable routing.

Talia Benson: Capturing this for the runbook. Can we agree on *alert thresholds* (page vs ticket)? Proposed:
P0 page:
- HubSpot 429s sustained (>= 10% of requests for 10m) OR retry queue depth continuously increasing for 15m
- End-to-end lag p95 > 6h (excluding backfills)
- Task create failures > 2% for 10m
P1 ticket:
- 5xx spikes but recovering
- row writes drop >50% vs 7d median
Thoughts?

Logan Wright: 6h lag feels high given we’re doing daily windows, but the expectation from sales is “I should see tasks same day.” I’d set:
- ticket at p95 > 2h for 2 consecutive hours
- page at p95 > 6h for 2 consecutive hours
and explicitly suppress during backfill.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

DeployBot: reminder: PR-28630 (metrics hooks) merged to main. Staging deploy scheduled 18:00 PT.

Logan Wright: Dashboard placeholders (will fill once Grafana panels are live):
- Signal Router Overview: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/overview (TBD)
- HubSpot Delivery: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/hubspot (TBD)
- Ingestion + DLQ: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/ingestion (TBD)
Runbook placeholder: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/PLG+Signal+Router+Oncall+Runbook (TBD)

Marcus Lin: +1 on runbook including “kill switch” steps if payload policy violations fire.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Ethan: define alert rules for HubSpot success rate + retry queue depth (and add to PD service)
- Mei/Caleb: freshness + DLQ alerts with backfill suppression
- Talia: create runbook skeleton + link to INT-1843 + suppression guidance
Let’s aim to have alerts in place before we expand pilot cohort next week.

Mei Lin: +1 to suppression during backfills. We can tag runs with `window_type=daily|backfill` and key alerts off daily only.
Also add:
- `aggregation_window_complete_timestamp` gauge (epoch) so we can compute freshness in Grafana easily.

Ethan Park: On 429s: HubSpot rate limits are bursty. I’d rather page on “routing stopped” symptoms:
- `hubspot_task_create_success_rate` < 95% for 15m (page)
- `hubspot_retry_queue_depth` > 5k and increasing (page)
And a warning when 429 ratio > 5% for 10m (ticket) so we tune batching.

Caleb Johnson: For dropped events: page if `telemetry_events_dropped_total{reason!=schema_invalid}` increases by > 1k in 10m AND lag p95 > 1h. Otherwise we’ll page every time a client SDK rolls.

Priya Natarajan: This is great. Let’s lock a minimal set for day-1 oncall:
1) HubSpot delivery health (success rate, retries, 429/5xx)
2) E2E lag
3) DLQ depth / dropped events
4) Job/run success
Anything beyond that can be phase 2.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a “volume sanity” panel:
- `signals_created_total` by type
- `signals_routed_total` by region
If we suddenly double our routing volume, that’s usually a threshold regression.

Talia Benson: Owners?
- HubSpot metrics/alerts: @Ethan Park
- Ingestion lag/DLQ/drops: @Caleb Johnson
- Aggregation freshness + writes: @Mei Lin
- Overall dashboards + PD integration: I can coordinate but need a DRI for the service

Priya Natarajan: DRI for service ops is Logan (Telemetry & Analytics) for this pilot. Ethan owns HubSpot path. I’ll be escalation for platform.

DeployBot: reminder: PR-28630 (metrics hooks) merged to main. Staging deploy scheduled 18:00 PT.

Logan Wright: Dashboard placeholders (will fill once Grafana panels are live):
- Signal Router Overview: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/overview (TBD)
- HubSpot Delivery: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/hubspot (TBD)
- Ingestion + DLQ: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/PLG_SIGNAL_ROUTER/ingestion (TBD)
Runbook placeholder: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/ENG/PLG+Signal+Router+Oncall+Runbook (TBD)

Marcus Lin: +1 on runbook including “kill switch” steps if payload policy violations fire.

Priya Natarajan: Action items:
- Ethan: define alert rules for HubSpot success rate + retry queue depth (and add to PD service)
- Mei/Caleb: freshness + DLQ alerts with backfill suppression
- Talia: create runbook skeleton + link to INT-1843 + suppression guidance
Let’s aim to have alerts in place before we expand pilot cohort next week.

title: #eng: 1746816155
preview:
Connor O'Brien: Heads up: airgap bundle from the latest private installer build is missing the Grafana dashboards for the Private observability min pack. On-prem install comes up, Prometheus scrapes OK, but Grafana has *zero* Redwood dashboards.

Fatima Noor: Confirmed on my side. Using the onprem offline flow (no egress). Grafana is reachable, datasource is configured, but dashboard folder is empty. I only see default Grafana stuff.

Diego Santos: Ugh. Is it just dashboards or also alert rules?

Fatima Noor: Alerts *seem* present (I see the PrometheusRule objects). The missing bit looks like the dashboard JSONs aren’t in the airgap tarball at all.

Dylan Brooks: That matches what I saw in staging earlier this week. The Helm chart references dashboards via ConfigMaps, but the installer’s airgap bundle needs to include the `observability-pack` dashboards directory (or the generated ConfigMap manifests). Might be a manifest omission in the bundler.

Chloe Martin: Quick check: are we expecting the installer to ship raw dashboard JSONs and render ConfigMaps at install-time, or ship pre-rendered ConfigMaps? I thought we went with “ship raw dashboards + grafana sidecar loads them”.

Diego Santos: For Private installer we’re packaging the raw JSON artifacts + the helm bits. If those JSONs aren’t in the bundle, nothing to mount, so sidecar has nothing to load.

Connor O'Brien: I unpacked the artifact and searched:
`tar -tf redwood-private-airgap-bundle.tgz | grep -i grafana`
Nothing except the Grafana image tar.

Rafael Mendes: That explains the Support thread we saw last week (“dashboards blank after upgrade”). If this is the same root cause, we need a hotfix fast.

Sahana Iyer: CI didn’t catch this because smoke tests run in connected env and pull dashboards from the repo path. Airgap path uses the bundle manifest only. We should add an assertion that the bundle contains at least N dashboard JSON files.

Logan Wright: +1. Also can we verify the bundle includes the alert rules + recording rules too? Would rather not fix dashboards and then find rules missing.

Connor O'Brien: Root cause looks like `airgap-bundle-manifest.yaml` doesn’t include the dashboards/rules artifacts from `observability-pack` (only includes images + helm charts). Probably got dropped in the last refactor.

Dylan Brooks: I can take a look at the bundler config. It should include:
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/dashboards/*.json`
- `observability-pack/private-minimum/prometheus-rules/*.yaml`
And then installer should copy into the expected `/var/lib/redwood/observability/` path.

Chloe Martin: If we add raw dashboards, make sure we preserve folder structure so Grafana sidecar labels/folders still work (Runtime vs Control Plane vs Infra). Otherwise everything lands in “General”.

Diego Santos: Also ensure checksums update. Airgap customers verify SHA256 from the manifest.

Connor O'Brien: Proposed plan:
1) Patch installer bundle manifest to include dashboards + rules
2) Cut a hotfix PR + backport to release branch
3) Trigger airgap build + rerun onprem validation plan (at least: install, upgrade, confirm dashboards load)

Rafael Mendes: For severity: I’d call this a release-blocker for on-prem. Without dashboards the “minimum pack” story is broken.

Fatima Noor: Agree. For regulated customers, they specifically asked for turnkey dashboards in offline env. This is exactly the failure mode.

Sahana Iyer: I’ll add a CI step to the installer pipeline:
- unpack bundle
- `find . -name "*.json" | grep dashboards | wc -l` must be > 0
- and maybe validate presence of a known dashboard UID/title file

Elliot Price: Once fixed, can someone drop me the exact version + any user-facing notes? Docs currently say “dashboards included in offline bundle” so we should add a known-issue note for the broken versions + the fixed version.

Connor O'Brien: Dylan can you own the PR? I’ll coordinate the backport + release note.

Dylan Brooks: Yep. I’ll open a PR to `redwood-private-installer` updating the airgap manifest + post-install validation to fail loudly if dashboards directory is missing.

Diego Santos: Please add a preflight warning too. Something like: “observability artifacts not present in bundle; dashboards will be empty” so we don’t silently ship a broken pack.

Chloe Martin: Can we also add a quick postinstall check that Grafana has loaded at least one dashboard ConfigMap? Even a simple “list ConfigMaps with grafana_dashboard=1 label” would help.

Dylan Brooks: Good call. I’ll add a postinstall validation step:
`kubectl -n <ns> get cm -l grafana_dashboard=1 | grep redwood || exit 1`

Logan Wright: While you’re in there, make sure rules path is included too. We had a similar miss earlier where recording rules weren’t copied.

Dylan Brooks: Will do. I’ll verify both dashboard JSONs and rule YAMLs are bundled.

CI Bot: Build notification: nightly private-installer airgap job #482 succeeded (artifact uploaded).

Rafael Mendes: Nightly succeeded but doesn’t validate contents. Let’s not rely on that.

Connor O'Brien: Tracking: Dylan PR today, backport tomorrow AM, then Fatima to re-run the onprem airgap validation checklist.

Fatima Noor: I can rerun as soon as we have a new bundle. I’ll test:
- fresh airgap install
- upgrade from previous broken bundle -> fixed bundle
- confirm dashboards render and alerts present

Elliot Price: Please capture screenshots or at least the list of dashboards showing up. Helps for Support enablement.

Dylan Brooks: PR incoming in ~1 hour.

Connor O'Brien: Thanks all. Let’s keep this thread updated with the PR link + the fixed build ID once it’s cut.

title: #eng-infra: 1746384109
preview:
Sean Gallagher: We need to land a default Prometheus TSDB sizing + retention rec for Private min pack. What are we comfortable shipping as "sane defaults" for typical footprints? Mostly concerned about PVC size + churn when folks have 15s scrapes.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Support keeps getting "Prometheus disk full" escalations, and we don’t have consistent guidance. Can we target 7d retention as default and document 15d/30d options?

Allison Grant: From standards side: minimum pack should be conservative on cardinality, but customers will still have node exporter + kube metrics + our runtime metrics. Default should prioritize stability over long retention. 7d time-based retention is fine, but we should also recommend `retention.size` as a guardrail.

Logan Wright: Agree. Also reminder: some Private installs use smaller clusters / single AZ w/ standard storage class. We should avoid recommending huge PVCs by default if we can.

Diego Santos: What are we calling "typical"? I’m seeing:
- small: 2–4 GPU nodes, <100 RPS
- medium: 8–20 GPU nodes, 100–500 RPS
- large: 50+ nodes, >500 RPS
Prometheus is mostly cluster metrics + runtime, not per-request.

Thomas Baird: Quick rule of thumb that’s worked: TSDB footprint ~ 1–3 bytes/sample on disk (varies), plus head/WAL overhead. In K8s installs, people underestimate churn (pods, containers, series churn). I’d budget 2x for head + compaction + WAL.

Sean Gallagher: Do we have any datapoints from our staging private cluster? Like actual series count + disk/day?

Sahana Iyer: From the smoke-test harness (single cluster, 3 serving pods, control plane, kube-state-metrics, node exporter) I saw ~180k active series once it settles. Disk growth was ~1.5–2.0GB/day at 15s scrapes w/ 7d retention, but that cluster is tiny.

Rafael Mendes: That’s helpful. Most customer clusters will have more nodes + more container metrics. Also some will turn on additional exporters (ingress-nginx, etc.).

Allison Grant: We should explicitly call out scrape interval expectations. If we’re assuming 15s and customer sets 5s, our sizing falls apart.

Connor O'Brien: Packaging-wise: helm values today default to 15s scrape in kube-prometheus-stack. We can document that changing it requires re-sizing PVC.

Thomas Baird: Another thing: enable `--storage.tsdb.wal-compression` by default. Helps w/ WAL size for write-heavy setups.

Logan Wright: +1 on WAL compression. Also I’m in favor of setting both:
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=7d`
- `--storage.tsdb.retention.size=80GB` (or similar)
So time-based retention doesn’t fill disks if there’s unexpected cardinality.

Sean Gallagher: What should the PVC be then? If we cap size at 80GB but PVC is 50Gi, we’re going to thrash earlier.

Thomas Baird: Prometheus needs free space for compaction and block writes. If you set retention.size near the PVC limit, you can still OOM/lock up on compaction.
Rule I use: retention.size <= ~60–70% of PVC. Rest is head/WAL/compaction buffer.

Rafael Mendes: Support reality: customers don’t want Prometheus consuming >200Gi unless we justify it.
Can we do defaults like:
- small: 50Gi PVC, 7d, retention.size 30Gi
- medium: 200Gi PVC, 15d, retention.size 120Gi
- large: 500Gi+, 15–30d depending on remote write
?

Allison Grant: That seems reasonable. For the minimum pack, we probably ship one default (small/medium-ish) and then provide a sizing table.

Diego Santos: I’d rather we ship a single default that won’t blow up on "medium". Maybe:
- default PVC: 100Gi
- default retention.time: 7d
- default retention.size: 60Gi
Then document: if you want 15d, go 200–300Gi.

Logan Wright: 100Gi default might be too big for some on-prem (local PVs are annoying), but too small for large clusters. Still probably the least-worst.

Sahana Iyer: FWIW, some air-gapped POCs have *tiny* storage classes. We got a question last week: “can I run this with 20Gi?”

Sean Gallagher: 20Gi feels like guaranteed pain unless you’re scraping almost nothing.

Thomas Baird: Yeah 20Gi will fill in a couple days once kube metrics are in. Also if storage is slow, compaction gets backed up.
We should add a note: “use SSD-backed storage; avoid networked HDD for Prometheus TSDB if possible.”

Rafael Mendes: Also: what about Grafana? People ask for "Grafana storage" too. It’s mostly sqlite + plugins. We can say 5–10Gi PVC is enough.

Allison Grant: +1. Grafana persistent volume mostly for dashboards/provisioning state. If dashboards are provisioned from configmaps, you can even run ephemeral, but let’s keep 10Gi default to be safe.

Connor O'Brien: In helm we already provision dashboards as configmaps. Grafana PVC is optional, but enabling persistence avoids losing user-created dashboards. Minimum pack probably shouldn’t assume custom dashboards though.

Sean Gallagher: OK, proposing we bake into doc + values:
Prometheus:
- PVC: 100Gi default
- retention.time: 7d
- retention.size: 60Gi
- wal-compression: true
And we publish a table (small/medium/large) with recommended PVC/retention.

Thomas Baird: I’d add one more knob: set requests/limits for Prometheus so it doesn’t get evicted under pressure. CPU spikes during compaction.
Maybe requests: 2 CPU / 8Gi mem, limits: 4 CPU / 16Gi mem for the default footprint.

Rafael Mendes: +1, we’ve seen OOMKills in customer clusters when compaction runs and memory is too low.

Sahana Iyer: In CI, Prometheus w/ 2Gi mem was flakey. 8Gi request stabilized it.

Allison Grant: Can we also include “cardinality guardrails” section: if series > X, you should reduce labels or drop high-churn metrics? That’ll help Support.

Logan Wright: Yes. Maybe a quick snippet to check series count:
`prometheus_tsdb_head_series`
And churn:
`rate(prometheus_tsdb_head_series_created_total[5m])`

Sean Gallagher: Great. I’ll update the sizing guidance draft with:
- default values
- sizing table (small/med/large)
- formulas + series/churn queries
- callouts about scrape interval + SSD storage

Connor O'Brien: Please also add a one-liner for installer preflight: warn if Prometheus PVC < 50Gi when min pack enabled.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: and add "if disk usage >85% page" guidance + runbook link. That’s the common incident path.

Sean Gallagher: ack. I’ll also add that `retention.size` should be <70% of PVC to leave compaction headroom.

Thomas Baird: One nit: Gi vs GB confusion. In values, it’s Gi. In docs, call it out explicitly.

Allison Grant: +1. People misconfigure and then wonder why it’s tight.

Sean Gallagher: Cool, I’ll push an update today and drop link here for review.

Sean Gallagher: (later) Draft updates are in the doc: added table + default `100Gi / 7d / 60Gi`. Please comment if you think 100Gi is too aggressive for default installs.

Rafael Mendes: Reviewed. Looks good. I added a comment to emphasize remote_write as the path for >15d retention instead of bumping TSDB forever.

Logan Wright: +1 on remote_write note. Otherwise folks treat Prometheus as their long-term store.

Allison Grant: Left minor edits on wording + added the series/churn queries. Approved from standards POV.

Connor O'Brien: From packaging POV: OK. Preflight warning threshold at 50Gi makes sense.

Sean Gallagher: :shipit: I’ll proceed with the defaults + docs and we can tweak if staging noise comes back.

title: #devex: 1740842011
preview:
Talia Benson: Hey all — pushing the launch brief into "ready to execute" today. Need final owner assignments + dates for the remaining launch tasks so I can lock the checklist + comms. Please reply in-thread with what you own + ETA.

Scope reminders for v1:
- Python + TS RAG+agent templates
- CLI: `init` + `doctor` (no in-place upgrade story for v1)
- observability pack dashboards + recommended alerts
- docs IA + walkthrough + troubleshooting
- support enablement + announcement

Owner buckets we need to nail:
1) Docs updates (IA/nav, walkthrough, troubleshooting)
