title: #devex: 1740831155
preview:
Monica Patel: Pulling together a list of SDK gaps that are actively blocking the quickstart templates (Python + TS) from feeling “production-minded by default”. Dropping here so we can track what’s must-fix before launch vs nice-to-have. Please add anything I missed.

Monica Patel: **TS SDK**
1) Streaming error types are too lossy. In templates we can’t reliably tell `timeout` vs `connection reset` vs `429` vs `5xx` during stream. Right now it’s basically `Error: fetch failed` and we lose status/code.
2) No clean “stream aborted by client” vs “server closed stream” distinction.
3) Reconnect strategy for streaming: we need a safe default pattern (e.g., retry on network disconnect before any tokens, but do not replay partial responses). SDK doesn’t expose hooks/metadata to do this sanely.
4) Lack of a stable request-id capture. We need Redwood request id (gateway header) to show up in logs/traces.

Monica Patel: **Python SDK**
1) Trace context propagation helpers are missing / awkward. Template instrumentation currently has to do custom glue to attach span context + request-id.
2) Timeout + retry defaults are not aligned with the safe-defaults doc (templates are doing overrides). Ideally SDK defaults match so templates don’t carry bespoke policy.
3) Streaming: when the generator errors mid-stream, we need better exception typing + access to response metadata (status, headers) for logging and support.

Monica Patel: **Cross-cutting**
- OTel-friendly hooks: we need a supported way to wrap client calls so templates can emit spans without monkeypatching.
- Template telemetry tags/headers: if we’re sending an opt-in header, SDK should preserve it and not drop/rename it. (Also need gateway whitelist; Brian/Logan tracking?)

Dana Lo: +1 on TS streaming. In the template I’m trying to do something like:
```ts
for await (const chunk of client.chat.completions.stream(...)) {
  // ...
}
```
When it blows up, it’s not actionable. We need `err.status`, `err.code`, and ideally `err.retryable`.

Ibrahim Khan: For Python, we can land helpers similar to what we did in other clients:
- `get_current_traceparent()` (or equivalent)
- capture `x-redwood-request-id` from responses
- structured errors: `AuthError`, `QuotaError`, `RateLimitError`, `NetworkError`, `TimeoutError`, `ServerError`
Then templates can map those to our error taxonomy + log fields.

Sabrina James: On tests side, missing error typing makes it hard to write CI smoke tests. Right now the streaming tests can’t assert “we got a 429 and backed off” vs “socket hung up”. Everything looks the same.

Brian Murphy: Re: request id + headers — gateway currently returns `x-redwood-request-id` consistently, but not all SDKs surface it. TS: fetch Response headers are there but we throw away the Response object. If we refactor the internals to keep `response` around, we can expose requestId. Same for Python.

Allison Grant: From observability POV, templates need:
- span attributes: `redwood.request_id`, `llm.provider`, `llm.model`, `llm.streaming=true/false`, `http.status_code`
- stable error classification for `error.type`
Right now templates are guessing. If SDK gives typed errors + request id, we can standardize.

Wei Chen: Adding doc pain: troubleshooting page needs to say “if you see X, do Y”. Without typed errors we can’t give deterministic guidance.

Logan Wright: Telemetry tagging: we need to be careful with headers. Some corp proxies reject unknown headers (we’ve seen this elsewhere). If templates are going to tag traffic, it should be **opt-in** and (ideally) via query param or a whitelisted header with a very conservative format.

Monica Patel: Totally. For launch I think we do opt-in only and default OFF. But even opt-in we need SDK to pass it through. Dana: are you using a custom header or metadata field?

Dana Lo: Currently passing a header like `x-redwood-template: rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` (example). But our internal client wrapper doesn’t consistently include it across calls, esp. the streaming path.

Brian Murphy: That specific header will need whitelist validation on gateway. Also, format: `rag-agent-ts@0.1.0` is fine, but we should restrict chars to avoid injection. Logan can advise.

Ibrahim Khan: For retries/backoff: templates currently implement their own. That’s risky. If we can align SDK defaults with safe defaults (timeouts, max attempts, jitter) we reduce drift + support load.

Monica Patel: Agree. Proposal for “must-fix before template launch” (v1):
- TS: streaming reconnect + error typing + request-id extraction
- Python: context propagation helpers + request-id extraction + retry/timeout defaults
- Both: OTel hooks (official way) so templates don’t patch internals

Sabrina James: Also: TS abort behavior. When user closes the HTTP connection to our template server, we should abort the upstream LLM call. Today we call `AbortController.abort()` but the SDK doesn’t always stop the stream; we leak work.

Dana Lo: Yes, that’s a big one. In Node, `fetch` / undici semantics are weird. We need the SDK to treat abort as first-class and stop reading/decoding.

Allison Grant: +1. Also would be good if “abort” is not logged as an error in traces. Otherwise dashboards show false incident spikes.

Ibrahim Khan: We can model abort as a distinct exception type and mark span status appropriately (`UNSET` or `OK` depending on conventions). We should align with OTel semantic convs.

Logan Wright: Quick note: attribute cardinality. Please do not add raw error messages or user IDs as span attributes. Request id is fine. Model name is fine. Any “template version” tag should be bounded.

Monica Patel: Action items:
- Ibrahim: can you write up a concrete Python SDK change list (APIs + error classes + request id surfacing) by EOW?
- Dana: can you open a TS SDK issue with a minimal reproduction for streaming errors + abort leaks?
- Brian/Logan: confirm header whitelist + recommended format if we keep template tagging opt-in.
- Allison: share the “minimum required attributes” list for templates so SDK hooks can set them consistently.

Dana Lo: Yep. I’ll file it and link to the template PR where we’re working around it.

Ibrahim Khan: Will do. I’ll also cross-check with the safe-defaults doc so we’re not inventing a new policy.

Brian Murphy: I’ll sync with gateway folks about whitelist constraints and whether we can do a query param instead of header.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop the attribute list + do/don’t for cardinality in this thread.

Wei Chen: Once we know the final error shapes, I can update troubleshooting copy with exact messages + remediation steps.

Monica Patel: Perfect. Goal is to stop papering over SDK gaps in templates. Templates should be thin glue + best practices, not a second SDK.

title: #devex: 1740708451
preview:
Monica Patel: quick q for @Allison Grant / @Logan Wright — we’re wiring OTel into the quickstart templates (RAG + agents) and want to make sure we don’t shoot ourselves w/ cardinality. what attrs/dimensions are “allowed” for spans + metrics? we have some ideas (model, template_version, tool_name, vector_store) but worried about exploding tags.

Allison Grant: +1 raising early. we’ve been burned before. general rule: spans can tolerate more context, metrics cannot. also: never put user input (prompt, query, doc IDs) into attrs.

Logan Wright: yep. metrics: keep label set small + bounded. if you can’t enumerate possible values in advance, it’s probably not safe for metrics.

Monica Patel: makes sense. current draft dims for metrics:
- `service.name` (template app)
- `deployment.mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `model` (string)
- `http.route`
- `tool.name`
- `retriever.backend` (faiss/local, etc)
- `template.version`

Is `model` and `tool.name` too risky?

Caleb Johnson: depends. `model` can be ok if it’s pinned to a small supported set and normalized (e.g. `rw/model_family` or `rw.model_id` from an allowlist). if users can type arbitrary model names, it will balloon.

Allison Grant: +100. for templates, we should strongly encourage model pinning and only emit `rw.model` from a bounded set (or map unknown -> `custom`).

Sergio Costa: `tool.name` in agent templates can also explode if users add 20 tools w/ unique names. maybe use `tool.type` (e.g. `http`, `db`, `vector_search`, `filesystem`) rather than name? or only allowlist built-in tools.

Dana Lo: in TS template we were planning to name tools after functions (`get_user_by_email`, etc). definitely unbounded.

Christine Wong: + re: `http.route` — make sure it’s the route template, not full URL w/ IDs (`/users/:id` vs `/users/123`). we should be careful in examples.

Monica Patel: good call. in FastAPI/Express we can use route pattern.

Wei Chen: doc side question: do we want to tell users “don’t add dynamic labels” explicitly in README? or just hide it.

Allison Grant: we should be explicit. “if it looks like a UUID, don’t put it in tags.” also: “prefer logs for high-card debugging.”

Logan Wright: for Redwood Console dashboards, we mainly need a few stable breakdowns:
- route
- status (success/error)
- error.type (bounded)
- deployment.mode
- template (name + version)
- model (bounded)
Everything else should go to traces/logs.

Monica Patel: can we align on a concrete list for v1? like “required attrs” + “allowed attrs”?

Caleb Johnson: suggestion: define 3 buckets:
1) REQUIRED (must emit)
2) RECOMMENDED (emit if available, bounded)
3) TRACE-ONLY (ok on spans but do not use as metric labels)

Allison Grant: +1. here’s a strawman.

Allison Grant: REQUIRED (spans + metrics):
- `service.name` / `service.version` (template app + git sha or template version)
- `deployment.environment` (dev/staging/prod)
- `rw.deployment_mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `http.method`
- `http.route`
- `http.response.status_code`
- `rw.request_id` (TRACE ONLY maybe? but at least in logs)

RECOMMENDED (bounded, OK for metrics):
- `rw.template_name` (e.g. `rag-agent-python`)
- `rw.template_version` (e.g. `0.4.2`)
- `rw.model` (allowlist or `custom`)
- `rw.stream` (true/false)
- `rw.cache` (hit/miss/na)
- `rw.error_type` (network/auth/quota/timeout/tool/retrieval/validation/unknown)

TRACE-ONLY (spans only, NOT metrics labels):
- `rw.tool_name` (string)
- `rw.vector_store_collection` (string)
- `rw.retrieved_doc_count` (numeric ok as span attr; as metric use histogram w/o labels)
- `rw.conversation_id` (NO for metrics, probably NO entirely unless hashed?)

Hard NO (neither):
- prompts, queries, doc IDs, raw URLs w/ query params, user IDs/emails, API keys (obv)

Thoughts?

Sergio Costa: for dashboards, having `rw.tool_name` would be *nice* but agree trace-only. we can still compute tool failure rate w/ `rw.tool_type` as a bounded set.

Dana Lo: we can add `tool.type` in our wrapper (http/db/vector). tool name stays in logs.

Ibrahim Khan: +1 on request_id: keep it out of metrics labels. add it to logs + trace attributes only. if we make it a metric label we’ll crash cardinality.

Logan Wright: agree. `rw.request_id` should be:
- trace attribute on the root span
- log field on every log line
- NOT a metric label

Christine Wong: also watch for `exception.message` and `error.message` attrs. OTel SDKs will happily attach giant strings. for templates, we should redact/limit length. helpful for debug but can be PII.

Allison Grant: yes. we have a redaction processor in the collector examples, but we should also sanitize in-app for logs.

Monica Patel: question on tokens metrics. we want `tokens_in`, `tokens_out`, latency, retry_count. any concerns?

Caleb Johnson: tokens are fine as numeric metrics (counter/histogram). don’t label by `prompt_template` or `customer_id`. keep labels same as above.

Sergio Costa: also, `model` label + token counters is super useful. but again: only if bounded.

Monica Patel: ok. we can enforce `RW_MODEL` to be one of a documented list and map unknown -> `custom`.

Wei Chen: we should reflect that in docs: “if you change the model to a custom ID, metrics will show model=custom.” prevents surprise.

Dana Lo: for `http.client.*` attrs: do we use standard semantic conventions? (we’re instrumenting Redwood SDK calls)

Allison Grant: use OTel semantic conv where possible (`http.request.method`, `url.scheme`, etc) but please avoid `url.full` if it includes query params. prefer `server.address` + `server.port` + `http.route`.

Logan Wright: also: `net.peer.name`/`server.address` is typically low-card (api.redwood.ai) but in private deployments it could be internal hostnames. still bounded-ish, but I’d rather not label metrics by it.

Monica Patel: got it. keep endpoint/region as trace-only.

Christine Wong: re: region — might be ok as bounded dimension if we have like 5 regions. but if private/on-prem can be arbitrary. default to trace-only.

Allison Grant: yep: `rw.region` trace-only, unless it’s from a known Redwood set.

Monica Patel: action items:
1) DevEx will update template instrumentation to match buckets above
2) add `tool.type` (bounded) + move `tool.name` to logs/trace only
3) normalize `rw.model` to allowlist|custom
4) ensure `http.route` uses patterns
5) add docs note on cardinality + “NO PII in attrs”

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: i’ll also send a short snippet for an allowlist helper + examples.

Sergio Costa: i can adjust the dashboard pack assumptions (no tool_name breakdown).

Allison Grant: please paste the final attr list into the observability standard doc once updated. also if anyone adds new labels later, run it by us.

Monica Patel: will do. thanks all.
text:
Monica Patel: quick q for @Allison Grant / @Logan Wright — we’re wiring OTel into the quickstart templates (RAG + agents) and want to make sure we don’t shoot ourselves w/ cardinality. what attrs/dimensions are “allowed” for spans + metrics? we have some ideas (model, template_version, tool_name, vector_store) but worried about exploding tags.

Allison Grant: +1 raising early. we’ve been burned before. general rule: spans can tolerate more context, metrics cannot. also: never put user input (prompt, query, doc IDs) into attrs.

Logan Wright: yep. metrics: keep label set small + bounded. if you can’t enumerate possible values in advance, it’s probably not safe for metrics.

Monica Patel: makes sense. current draft dims for metrics:
- `service.name` (template app)
- `deployment.mode` (hosted/dedicated/private)
- `model` (string)
- `http.route`
- `tool.name`
- `retriever.backend` (faiss/local, etc)
- `template.version`

Is `model` and `tool.name` too risky?

Caleb Johnson: depends. `model` can be ok if it’s pinned to a small supported set and normalized (e.g. `rw/model_family` or `rw.model_id` from an allowlist). if users can type arbitrary model names, it will balloon.

Allison Grant: +100. for templates, we should strongly encourage model pinning and only emit `rw.model` from a bounded set (or map unknown -> `custom`).

Sergio Costa: `tool.name` in agent templates can also explode if users add 20 tools w/ unique names. maybe use `tool.type` (e.g. `http`, `db`, `vector_search`, `filesystem`) rather than name? or only allowlist built-in tools.

Dana Lo: in TS template we were planning to name tools after functions (`get_user_by_email`, etc). definitely unbounded.

Christine Wong: + re: `http.route` — make sure it’s the route template, not full URL w/ IDs (`/users/:id` vs `/users/123`). we should be careful in examples.

Monica Patel: good call. in FastAPI/Express we can use route pattern.

Wei Chen: doc side question: do we want to tell users “don’t add dynamic labels” explicitly in README? or just hide it.

Allison Grant: we should be explicit. “if it looks like a UUID, don’t put it in tags.” also: “prefer logs for high-card debugging.”

Logan Wright: for Redwood Console dashboards, we mainly need a few stable breakdowns:
- route
- status (success/error)
- error.type (bounded)
- deployment.mode
- template (name + version)
- model (bounded)
Everything else should go to traces/logs.

- `rw.tool_name` (string)
- `rw.vector_store_collection` (string)
- `rw.retrieved_doc_count` (numeric ok as span attr; as metric use histogram w/o labels)
- `rw.conversation_id` (NO for metrics, probably NO entirely unless hashed?)

Dana Lo: we can add `tool.type` in our wrapper (http/db/vector). tool name stays in logs.

title: #eng-oncall: 1740604722
preview:
Miles Thornton: want to lock down “degraded mode” behavior for incident-bot v2 before we ship to broader oncall. Specifically: what we do when deploy svc/config diff svc/telemetry queries are slow/unhealthy. Please add thoughts / +1s.

Miles Thornton: my proposal (high level):
- Bot should always respond quickly w/ *something* (even if it’s just links + cached context)
- Make it obvious when data is stale/partial
- Provide a small “manual steps” snippet so oncall isn’t stuck

Rafael Mendes: +1. last incident we lost 7-10 min just waiting on telemetry query retries and then people assumed “no data” == “no issue”. Needs explicit “couldn’t fetch” + next step.

Devon Price: constraints reminder: Slack interactivity requires ack in 3s. We can send an initial “working on it” and then update message, but we should avoid long-running flows in the interaction handler.

Dylan Brooks: yep. we should treat each “card” as best-effort w/ its own timeout and never block the whole thread context.

Jacob Stein: concrete suggestion:
- overall context render budget: 6s wall clock
- per-card query budget: 1.5s (deploy), 2s (telemetry), 2s (config diff)
- if a card misses budget, show degraded placeholder immediately + link button.

Logan Wright: telemetry query svc can spike to 5-10s under incident load (or worse). please do not retry synchronously. if you do retries, do async and update card later.

Allison Grant: + “degraded mode” should avoid hammering metrics. if we’re already in an incident w/ high 5xx, bot calling metrics endpoints repeatedly can be self-inflicted. i’d prefer:
- 1 live attempt
- then cached
- then links only

Miles Thornton: ok, so: 1 live attempt (short timeout), then cached fallback. No blocking retries.

Mina Haddad: and for routing/fallback context: if control plane is flaky, showing *last known routing policy* is better than nothing, but must be labeled as such. Also ensure links land on correct service/region/time window even if stale.

Ravi Menon: important UX nuance: responders need to trust the bot. So the degraded message should be explicit:
- “Live fetch failed (timeout)” vs “No changes detected”
- “Cached as of <ts>”
- “Click to refresh (may be slow)”

Aisha Bello: for “manual steps” snippet, keep it short and safe. 3-4 commands max + the right dashboard link. Also make sure it’s generic across Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Connor O'Brien: deploy metadata side: if deploy service is down, can we fall back to the deploy bot feed / last deploy event in the incident channel? (not perfect but better than blank)

Dylan Brooks: we can’t reliably query Slack history w/ our current bot scopes (and it’s noisy). Better: cache last successful deploy context by service/region for ~15 min.

Devon Price: +1. we already have caching PR up. we can store payload keyed by (service, region, env) w/ TTL.

Miles Thornton: let’s define a simple degraded-mode spec for v2:

Miles Thornton: **Degraded mode behavior (draft):**
1) **Always post a context message within ~3s** (Slack ack safe). If live data isn’t ready, post skeleton cards with “Fetching…” and then fill in via message update.
2) **Hard timeouts**:
   - Deploy context: 1.5s
   - Config diff: 2.0s
   - Saturation/telemetry: 2.0s
   - Total: 6s (then stop and render degraded placeholders)
3) **No synchronous retries**. If a query times out/5xx, do *one* async retry (max 1) and update card if it succeeds within the next ~30s; otherwise leave as degraded.
4) **Fallback order** per card:
   - Live fetch (bounded)
   - Cached result (if <15m old)
   - Links-only + manual steps
5) **User-facing labeling**:
   - If cached: “Cached as of <time> (may be stale)”
   - If failure: “Live fetch failed: <timeout/5xx/permission>”
   - If partial: “Only showing <X>/<Y> signals (others unavailable)”
6) **Buttons/actions**:
   - “Retry live fetch” (rate limited; disables itself for 60s per thread)
   - “Open dashboards” (always available)
   - “Copy manual steps” (always available)
7) **Load shedding trigger**: if telemetry query service returns “over budget” / circuit open, bot immediately switches telemetry card to links-only (no live attempt) for next 10 min (global flag), but still posts cached values if present.

Rafael Mendes: this is good. add one thing: when bot is degraded, it should *not* spam thread w/ repeated error updates. one update max per card.

Devon Price: +1. we can do: initial skeleton -> one update w/ final state. If async retry succeeds, update once; else no further edits.

Logan Wright: for telemetry specifically: please differentiate “timeout” vs “query rejected (budget)” vs “backend unhealthy”. We can return distinct error codes.

Allison Grant: and in the links-only state, link to the standard dashboards w/ params:
- service
- region
- last 30m window
- if tenant provided, include tenant filter *only if safe*

Jacob Stein: config diff: if authz fails (missing scope) vs service down, manual steps differ. authz failure should say “request break-glass / page platform oncall” not “try again”.

Mina Haddad: +1. for permission errors, show:
- “Access denied (scope): config.diff.read”
- “Escalate: #eng-platform-oncall”
- and still show safe, non-sensitive routing summary if available (like model pin / fallback enabled flags) but no diff.

Dylan Brooks: can we add a tiny table of failure modes -> UX?

Miles Thornton: yes, like:
- timeout/5xx => cached if available + “Retry live fetch”
- circuit open => links-only + cached
- permission denied => no retry button, show escalation
- invalid params => show bug message + link to file issue

Aisha Bello: manual steps snippet suggestion (generic):
- “Check error budget burn for service/region” link
- “Check saturation overview” link
- “Check recent deploy timeline” link
- One CLI example to pull top errors (if we have a standard)

Devon Price: we can embed commands like:
`rwctl incidents context --service <svc> --region <rgn> --last 30m` (if we have it)
But we don’t today.

Jacob Stein: we do have a couple internal curl endpoints folks use. But I’d rather not hardcode anything that changes.

Rafael Mendes: simplest: “Manual steps” should be mostly links + a reminder of who to page.

Miles Thornton: ok, manual steps v1 = links + “if missing, do X”.

Connor O'Brien: for deploy context card, manual steps can include: “Open Deployments (service) in Console” and “Search recent PRs by commit SHA” if we have it.

Dylan Brooks: also: ensure bot works during partial Slack issues (message update failures). If update fails, ok to post a follow-up message once w/ the card data.

Devon Price: yep. implement: try chat.update; if it errors w/ `message_not_found` or transient, fallback to chat.postMessage (one time). Will keep it minimal.

Miles Thornton: decisions I’m hearing (please react):
- :white_check_mark: Always respond w/in ~3s with skeleton context
- :white_check_mark: Per-card timeouts + total budget
- :white_check_mark: Live -> cached -> links/manual steps
- :white_check_mark: Clear labeling for stale/failed/partial
- :white_check_mark: No noisy retries; at most one async retry + one update
- :white_check_mark: Permission errors: no retry, show escalation

Allison Grant: :thumbsup: (also please ensure caching doesn’t blow up cardinality; key only by service/region/env, not tenant, unless we’re sure it’s safe)

Logan Wright: :thumbsup: telemetry side ok. we’ll expose “budget_rejected” so you can flip to links-only quickly.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: for routing/policy links, include “last known good” indicator.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: pls add to playbook too. Oncall needs to know what degraded means.

Miles Thornton: ok I’ll summarize in design doc + add acceptance criteria to ENG-5429/5427/5425. Devon/Dylan can you take implementation details.

Devon Price: yup. will add:
- retry button cooldown
- one-update rule
- degraded banner + cached timestamp

Dylan Brooks: + will make sure failure reasons are mapped consistently across cards.

Miles Thornton: closing thread once I paste this into the runbook doc. thanks all.

title: #eng-infra: 1740259101
preview:
Logan Wright: Heads up / quick retro from the Sev1 earlier — telemetry query latency spiked hard right when incident-bot started pulling saturation cards.

Grafana Bot: Alert triggered: telemetry-query-service p95_latency_ms > 5000 for 10m (current: 8120ms). Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/telemetry-query-service

Rafael Mendes: +1, oncall perspective: bot was timing out and then responders started manually refreshing dashboards -> made it worse. Need guardrails.

Miles Thornton: I pulled logs from incident-bot. We went from ~3 qps to ~70 qps in ~2 mins when people hit “Refresh signals” in multiple threads. Also Slack interactive actions retried a few times when ack was late.

Devon Price: ack deadline for Slack interactive is 3s; we were doing live query before ack in a couple paths. That causes retry storms. We can flip to ack-immediately + async update, but still need query cost controls.

Nadia Rahman: Looking at query service traces — the expensive ones are the “regional saturation overview” fanout queries. They expand to ~18 metric series per region * pool * model, and then we do 5m + 30m windows. During the incident, cardinality was already elevated.

Caleb Johnson: Yep, and some are uncached due to tenant param being part of cache key even when query is region-level. So we miss cache and re-run same query N times.

Jacob Stein: This aligns with what we saw: bot card mostly needs the *summary* (e.g., GPU util high + queue depth high + KV cache pressure) and then links to deeper dashboards. We shouldn’t compute the whole dashboard query set inline.

Aisha Bello: +1. Also: we should define a query budget for incident-bot per thread + per workspace. If it exceeds budget, bot should fall back to cached context and post deep links.

Allison Grant: For “fallback to links” behavior, can we standardize the deep links and time windows? During incident I saw people open dashboards with wrong time range.

Dylan Brooks: Proposal:
1) Add request coalescing in telemetry-query-service for identical queries (keyed by normalized query + time bucket).
2) Add cache with small TTL (30-60s) + stale-while-revalidate for bot use.
3) Enforce hard limits: max fanout, max series returned, and max query time (e.g., 2s) for bot scope.
4) If any limit hits: return partial + “degraded mode” payload with links.

Rafael Mendes: What do we mean by “normalized query”? We need to ensure it collapses equivalent requests across threads. Eg same region/service but different incident id.

Caleb Johnson: Normalize away incident_id/thread_id; round end_time to nearest 30s bucket; enforce fixed lookback windows for bot (5m, 30m). Also canonicalize label filters ordering.

Nadia Rahman: Another thing: query service currently lets callers ask arbitrary window. Bot sometimes requested 2h because it used “incident start” which changed as people edited the incident doc. Should pin to a fixed set of windows.

Miles Thornton: On bot side: we can remove “Refresh signals” from v2 cards during active incident unless you’re in a whitelisted oncall role. Or throttle it (1 refresh / 60s / thread).

Devon Price: We should also make refresh idempotent + ignore repeated clicks while one is in-flight. Slack UI feels like “nothing happened” so folks click 5x.

Logan Wright: I’m also worried about the query service being in the same blast radius. During incidents, telemetry is often degraded. Bot should not depend on it being healthy to be useful.

Jacob Stein: Exactly. For saturation card we can do:
- Prefer cached last-known-good for region/pool summary
- If live query >2s, stop + show cached + “data may be stale”
- Always attach links to dashboards + traces/logs for drilldown

Allison Grant: Re links: we have agreed formats in the incident-context guidelines draft. Needs to include service, region, and an explicit time window (now-30m..now). Also a “copy query” for power users.

Aisha Bello: Also want alerts tuned so we don’t page on the *telemetry tool* being slow unless it impacts SLO diagnosis broadly. But we do need a canary to detect when bot queries are slow (separate from generic query load).

Dylan Brooks: There’s already a ticket in flight for caching/coalescing (ENG-5427). We should add explicit “bot budget” enforcement + circuit breaker semantics.

Nadia Rahman: Circuit breaker idea: if p95 > X for Y minutes, query service returns 429-like “budget exceeded” to bot scope, and bot flips to link-only mode automatically. Avoid death spiral.

Rafael Mendes: +1. In the incident, responders were basically DDoSing telemetry without knowing. A clear message in the bot card would have helped.

Incident Bot: :warning: Some diagnostics may be incomplete due to telemetry query latency. Try dashboard links or rerun later.

Miles Thornton: Can we make that message more actionable?
Something like: “Telemetry is slow (p95 8s). Showing cached signals from 2m ago. For live data: open Saturation Overview dashboard (region prefilled).”

Devon Price: Yes. Also: we should ack Slack instantly and then update message when ready. If it’s degraded, update with that explanation. No retries.

Caleb Johnson: One more culprit: a couple queries were pulling scheduler backpressure with high-cardinality labels (fallback routing tags). I saw series count jump 10x. There was a recent hotfix PR to reduce that cardinality; ensure it’s deployed everywhere.

Logan Wright: Noted. I’ll check rollout status w/ release eng. Also, action items below.

Logan Wright: Action items (proposed):
- Telemetry team (Nadia/Caleb): implement query normalization + coalescing + TTL cache + stale-while-revalidate for bot scope. Add max-series + max-fanout + max-runtime enforcement. (maps to ENG-5427 + new subtask)
- Bot team (Miles/Devon/Dylan): ack-first, in-flight dedupe, per-thread refresh throttle, and degraded-mode messaging w/ links. (maps to ENG-5425 + ENG-5429)
- Observability (Allison/Aisha): confirm link formats/time windows + add perf canary for bot query latency and alert route. (maps to perf-canary PR)

Aisha Bello: Looks good. For budgets, can we define something concrete?
E.g. per incident thread: <= 10 live queries / 5 min; per workspace: <= 100 live queries / 5 min; else cached/links.

Nadia Rahman: Reasonable. We should enforce server-side too (query service) so bots can’t bypass.

Caleb Johnson: +1. Also separate budgets for tenant-aware queries (more expensive) vs region-level.

Rafael Mendes: For SRE: please add a snippet to oncall playbook: if bot shows degraded mode, don’t spam refresh; use links; if needed, run manual CLI which is rate-limited separately.

Jacob Stein: I’ll update the runbook section for “telemetry degraded” + what the saturation card means when cached.

Allison Grant: I’ll sync with Chloe on Console deep links. Need to ensure links land on the right region/pool and default to now-30m.

Dylan Brooks: I’ll open the additional Linear subtask for query budgets + degraded-mode contract between bot and query service.

Logan Wright: Thanks all. Let’s aim to have the cache/coalesce + bot throttle in before v2 broad rollout. This was a good warning shot.

title: #eng-platform: 1740082033
preview:
Cole Summers: Heads up: while wiring burst metering -> billing pipeline, I’m seeing a few edge cases around retries/idempotency that we should nail down before we tell Finance “numbers are clean”.

Cole Summers: Current plan (per PRD) is we emit `BurstUsageEvent` per request w/ fields: tenant_id, region, hw_class, model, burst_gpu_ms, tokens_out, tokens_in, admitted=true/false, denial_reason. The retry problem: gateway retries on 5xx/timeout and we can double-count if we emit on both attempts.

Nadia Rahman: +1. Billing pipeline today assumes events are append-only and unique by event_id. If we can’t guarantee uniqueness, reconciliation gets ugly fast.

Ethan Park: Can we rely on existing request ids from gateway? We have `x-redwood-request-id` (uuid) + optional `x-idempotency-key` (customer supplied) in Hosted. Dedicated gateway also sets a stable request id per attempt but I don’t think it’s stable across retries yet.

Benji Okafor: For Dedicated, we have envoy generating `x-request-id` per hop; it changes on retry unless we pin it. We can add a separate header that survives retry (generated once at edge).

Jordan Lee: Also need to be clear what “retry” means for burst. Is the unit we bill for “admitted capacity” or “actually consumed GPU time”? If we bill on admit but the request never executes (timeout before dispatch), that’s a mismatch.

Cole Summers: Exactly. I think we should only meter when the request actually starts execution on a burst GPU (or when the scheduler assigns burst slot). Not on admit.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we can emit a “start” and “end” event (or a single end event w/ duration). But we have to keep label cardinality under control. Event id should be a field in payload, not a metric label.

Allison Grant: Please don’t shove request_id into Prometheus labels :slightly_smiling_face: We can do logs/OTel events for per-request audit and keep Prom metrics aggregate.

Cole Summers: Proposal:
1) Introduce `burst_metering_key` stable across gateway retries.
2) Billing event_id = hash(tenant_id + burst_metering_key + burst_phase)
3) Emit usage only from data plane (scheduler/runtime) once we have actual GPU-ms.

Cole Summers: For #2, “burst_phase” might just be “usage_v1” so we don’t accidentally treat later schema as dup.

Nadia Rahman: If we can get a stable key, billing pipeline can do idempotent upsert / dedupe (we currently do exactly-once-ish w/ Kafka + consumer offsets, but retries upstream break assumptions). If you give me `event_id`, we can enforce uniqueness at sink.

Rishi Malhotra: Jumping in — from Finance side we care about two things:
- Customer invoice line items match the contract definition (e.g., burst GPU-seconds or burst token surcharge).
- Daily revenue reporting matches invoiced totals (no phantom usage).
If we tell customers “burst usage is measured by GPU-seconds actually consumed” then you must not charge on admit.

Laura Bennett: +1. Also we need explainability when a customer disputes: “here are the requests/intervals that contributed.” So having per-request audit trail is good, but invoicing is aggregate.

Ethan Park: On stable key: we can add `x-redwood-billing-id` generated at edge (once) and propagated through retries. Similar to idempotency, but platform-owned.

Benji Okafor: Implementation detail: envoy retry filter doesn’t preserve generated headers by default if upstream resets? We should set it explicitly in gateway code before it hits envoy retries.

Jordan Lee: Another edge case: if admission controller returns “queued” vs “denied”, request might sit, then client times out and retries. We might end up executing both unless we cancel old one. That’s not just billing; that’s correctness.

Cole Summers: For v1 we’re doing “immediate admit/deny” (no queue) per ADR draft, right? So less risk. But we still have internal retries (data plane transient errors) that can re-run dispatch.

Ethan Park: Correct: immediate admit/deny for GA, no customer-visible queue semantics. Internal backpressure still exists but request either goes through baseline or burst or gets 429.

Nadia Rahman: Then we can scope idempotency to: same customer request retried at gateway layer, or data plane “attempts” for the same request.

Logan Wright: Data plane can generate an “execution_id” per attempt. If we have `billing_id` stable, we can guarantee only one execution contributes to billing by taking the first successful execution.

Cole Summers: Yup. Something like:
- stable `billing_id` at edge
- `attempt` increments per internal retry
- emit usage event only for `terminal_state=success|partial_success` with GPU-ms measured
- if multiple success events show up (bug), billing sink dedupes by (tenant_id, billing_id)

Allison Grant: Define what “partial_success” means for streaming. If we stream tokens and then disconnect, GPU time was still consumed. Do we bill that?

Rishi Malhotra: We generally bill for work performed. If we generated 2k tokens and customer disconnected, that’s still cost. So yes, bill what’s actually generated.

Cole Summers: That means metering should be based on server-side token counts + GPU-ms, not client receipt.

Laura Bennett: Contract language needs to be precise: “usage based on Redwood-measured compute/time and tokens generated” etc. Michael/Sofia are drafting but we should sync them on these semantics.

Jordan Lee: Another edge: denied burst but served on baseline. That should not create burst usage. But we might still emit a “denial” event for observability.

Nadia Rahman: Denial events should NOT go into billing sink unless we explicitly separate streams. Otherwise someone will accidentally sum admitted=false events.

Cole Summers: Good call. We can split:
- `BurstUsageEvent` (billable) emitted only when burst pool resources consumed
- `BurstDecisionEvent` (non-billable) for analytics/ops

Logan Wright: Please also keep schema versioned. If you change field names, downstream breaks. Add `schema_version` string.

Ethan Park: What about reconciliation with Finance reporting? Rishi asked daily revenue totals.

Nadia Rahman: We can provide a daily rollup job per tenant:
- sum(burst_gpu_ms) by tenant/day/region/hw_class
- compare to invoice line items produced by billing service
- alert if delta > threshold (e.g. 0.5% or $50)
But again, this only works if upstream events are stable/idempotent.

Cole Summers: Can we get a “replay window” expectation? If we detect a bug, can we re-export from raw logs?

Nadia Rahman: Yes, but it’s painful. If we store raw usage events in the data lake (S3) with partition by day, we can reprocess. I’d rather not rely on that for GA correctness.

Benji Okafor: Action items I see:
1) Add `x-redwood-billing-id` generation at edge + propagate through routing/data plane.
2) Update metering emitter to use billing-id for event_id.
3) Split decision vs usage events.

Allison Grant: + add a short doc in the runbook: “customer disputes burst charges” -> where to pull per-request breakdown (console export or internal debug endpoint).

Cole Summers: I’ll write up a quick design note + update ENG-4827 w/ these semantics. Also going to add a unit test: simulate retry path (same billing-id, two attempts) => exactly 1 billing record.

Rishi Malhotra: Please tag me once you have the definition in writing. Finance sign-off depends on “metering unit” + reconciliation story.

BillingPipelineBot: Reminder: billing-data-prod nightly validation runs at 02:00 UTC. Current dedupe key: `event_id`. If `event_id` is empty or not unique, duplicates will be counted.

Laura Bennett: Thanks all. Once we have the stable billing-id approach, I’m comfortable telling Legal “we can defend the measurement.”

Cole Summers: Cool. I’ll loop in Michael/Sofia separately. Closing loop here after I push the header + dedupe changes.

title: #eng-oncall: 1739827209
preview:
Miles Thornton: looking for quick feedback from folks who’ve used the v2 beta cards this week. are they too verbose? too sparse? what would you change for “first 5 min” context?

Aisha Bello: i like having it all, but the saturation card is a wall of text in Slack. i end up scrolling past it to get to the actual incident thread.

Jacob Stein: +1. the “config diff” is the worst offender. in one incident it dumped like 80 lines (even with redaction) and it drowned out the human updates.

Devon Price: yep Slack is also not super forgiving. Block Kit sections start getting gnarly once we’re > ~3k chars. we’re doing some truncation but it’s not opinionated enough.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall: default should be *summary only* with one-click expand. i want to know:
- did anything deploy in last ~60m for this service/region
- did config/routing change recently
- are we saturated (gpu/queue)
then links.

Mina Haddad: agreed. also the routing bits should highlight only the stuff that affects traffic decisions:
- fallback enabled/disabled
- model pin changes
- weight shifts / route policy changes
- quota / rate limit changes
not every field in the config tree.

Logan Wright: saturation card: prefer “top 3 signals” + whether they crossed threshold + trend arrow. raw numbers for everything is noisy.

Miles Thornton: can you be specific about “top 3”? which ones do you actually look at?

Logan Wright: in practice:
1) queue depth / admission control (if it’s growing, that’s the story)
2) gpu util (but only meaningful w/ queue)
3) kv cache pressure / evictions (if we’re churning)
then link to the saturation overview dashboard. everything else can be behind “details”.

Aisha Bello: + include error budget burn link per region. i keep hopping between 2-3 dashboards to confirm it’s not just a noisy p95.

Chloe Martin: deep link wise: please make sure the links land me in Console with the right filters already set. biggest pain today is “dashboard link” opens global view and i lose 60 seconds clicking around.

Jacob Stein: + the links should lock the time window to incident start-ish. like “last 30m” or “since thread created”. if it opens “last 24h” it’s not helpful.

Devon Price: we can do both:
- default time window = `thread_ts - 30m to now`
- include a secondary link “open last 24h” for context

Mina Haddad: another thing: truncation rules need to be deterministic. i saw a diff where the interesting change got cut off but a bunch of unchanged metadata made it in.

Devon Price: what’s the ideal truncation heuristic?

Mina Haddad: for config diff:
- show only changed paths (no surrounding context)
- cap at e.g. 10 changed paths
- if > 10, show “+N more changes” and force modal
- within a path, show only old/new scalar values, don’t dump full nested objects

Jacob Stein: + categorize changes. like:
“Routing policy: 2 changes”
“Feature flags: 1 change”
“Quotas: 3 changes”
then list the specific ones under each. easier to scan.

Aisha Bello: and give a redaction warning that’s not scary but clear. right now it says “REDACTED” a lot and ppl assume the bot is broken.

Miles Thornton: +1. people asked me if it was failing because half the diff says `[REDACTED]`.

Devon Price: we can adjust copy to: “Sensitive values hidden. Use break-glass to view full diff (audited).”

Rafael Mendes: please don’t push break-glass in the happy path. most incidents don’t need it. just make sure summary contains the non-sensitive part.

Chloe Martin: deep links i want per card:
- Deploys: link to deploy timeline (service + region + last 2h) + link to PR/commit
- Config diff: link to “Config changes” view for that tenant/env (but safe) + link to request elevated access
- Saturation: link to “Capacity & saturation” dashboard with pool/region preselected
- Errors: link to “SLO burn” + “Top 5 error routes”

Logan Wright: + for saturation: add a “copy query” action if possible. i sometimes want to run the exact PromQL/NRQL with tweaks.

Devon Price: Slack action buttons can do “Copy to clipboard” only in modals (not always), but we can show a code block with the query + “open in Grafana” link.

incident-bot: :information_source: FYI Slack message payload limit hit; long diffs will be moved to a modal automatically.

Jacob Stein: lol that’s what we want :thumbsup:

Miles Thornton: what about the “recent deploys” card — is it too sparse now?

Aisha Bello: deploy card is mostly good. only tweak: if there were no deploys, say “No deploys detected for service X in region Y in last 60m” (explicit) so i don’t wonder if it failed to query.

Rafael Mendes: + add canary status *only if* canary exists. otherwise it’s confusing. i saw “Canary: N/A” and wasted time thinking there was supposed to be one.

Devon Price: got it. we’ll hide canary row when not applicable.

Mina Haddad: also: if there *was* a deploy but it’s in staged rollout, show rollout phase clearly (ex: “Phase 1/3 - 10% EU-West only”). that’s actually the key triage clue.

Miles Thornton: cool. summarizing proposed changes:
- summary-first cards, details behind modal
- config diff truncation by changed paths, cap 10, group by category
- saturation: highlight queue/gpu/kv w/ thresholds + trend; details behind modal
- deep links must prefilter service/region/tenant and time window anchored to thread
- explicit “no data” vs “query failed” messaging

Dylan Brooks: +1 from tooling side. please also keep card height consistent. if saturation explodes the layout it pushes human updates below the fold.

Devon Price: i’ll cut a ticket for:
1) deterministic truncation + grouping
2) link parameterization w/ thread_ts window
3) “no data” vs “error” states

Miles Thornton: thanks all. keep feedback coming esp. with screenshots from real incidents.

Aisha Bello: will do. i’ll drop a snippet next time. :pray:

title: #eng-oncall: 1739563228
preview:
Rafael Mendes: on-call handoff for audit exporter incident from earlier (lag spike / downstream 429s). capturing what to watch + toggles. cc @Aisha Bello (taking primary)

Redwood Incident Bot: Incident INC-4471 status changed: MITIGATED → MONITORING

Rafael Mendes: TL;DR
- root cause: Splunk HEC rate-limiting (429) on 2 beta tenants → retries/backoff built up queue → export lag peaked ~38m
- mitigation: paused Splunk adapter for affected tenants + reduced batch size + verified no DLQ growth
- current: lag back < 5m; retry rate normalizing

Rafael Mendes: Dashboards to keep open tonight:
1) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Overview (lag + throughput)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-overview
   Panels to watch:
   - "p95 export lag (by destination)"
   - "events exported/sec (by tenant)"
   - "checkpoint age"
2) Grafana: Audit Exporter / Destinations
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-destinations
   Panels:
   - "adapter HTTP status codes" (look for sustained 429/403)
   - "retry attempts" + "backoff seconds"
3) Queue/Storage dashboard (exporter internal)
   https://grafana.redwood.internal/d/audit-exporter-queues
   Panels:
   - "pending batches" (should not trend up)
   - "DLQ depth" (should be ~0; anything non-zero means investigate)

Aisha Bello: got it. what are the paging thresholds we’re using right now?

Rafael Mendes: from SRE draft (we used these in the incident):
- page if p95 export lag > 15m for 10m (any destination) OR > 30m for 5m (critical)
- page if delivery success rate < 99.5% over 30m (per adapter)
- ticket-only if 429s elevated but lag < 10m
(Sean can confirm if we’re tweaking, but this is what alert rules are set to as of today)

Sean Gallagher: confirming. alert rules match. note: we loosened 429 warning to ticket-only after the noise last week.

Rafael Mendes: Toggles / controls (use sparingly; prefer tenant-scoped first):
A) Pause exports per destination type (safe):
- control-plane admin: `POST /internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/pause` with body `{"destination":"splunk"}`
- UI (Console → Audit Exports → Actions → Pause) works if you have the SRE role
This stops new sends but keeps checkpoints; lag metric will stop increasing but backlog remains.

B) Global kill switch (last resort):
- feature flag: `siem_audit_export_beta_enabled` (tenant allowlist). DO NOT disable globally unless we’re melting down.
- if you must, flip via internal admin tool: https://admin.redwood.internal/flags/siem_audit_export_beta_enabled

C) Backpressure safety / auto-pause:
- exporter has `maxLagAutoPauseMinutes=60` (from PR-1119). If lag hits this, it auto-pauses the destination and emits an error.
- when it triggers, you’ll see alert "destination auto-paused" + log line `AUTO_PAUSE_MAX_LAG`.

Aisha Bello: if we pause, what’s the expected customer impact message?

Rafael Mendes: "exports temporarily paused due to downstream rate limiting; no audit events deleted; once resumed exporter will backfill from last checkpoint; duplicates possible (at-least-once)". don’t promise exact timestamps.

Logan Wright: adding: if customer asks "missing events", point them to dedup via `event_id` on their side. at-least-once semantics.

Rafael Mendes: Quick triage checklist if lag starts climbing again:
1) Identify adapter causing it:
   - check "p95 export lag by destination" + "HTTP status codes"
2) If 429s from Splunk:
   - confirm which tenant(s): panel "429s by tenant"
   - reduce pressure first (tenant pause Splunk) rather than global
3) If 403s from Datadog:
   - usually API key scope / wrong site (datadoghq.com vs datadoghq.eu). see SUP-18497 notes.
4) If S3 errors:
   - look for `AccessDenied` or KMS issues; often rotated role trust or KMS key policy.

Rafael Mendes: Commands / where to look:
- exporter logs (prod):
  `kubectl -n audit-exporter logs deploy/audit-exporter -c exporter --since=30m | rg "ERROR|429|AUTO_PAUSE|DLQ"`
- check tenant config state:
  `curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" https://control-plane.redwood.internal/internal/audit-exports/{tenant_id}/status`
  (status shows last_success_ts, last_error, paused=true/false, checkpoint_ts)

Aisha Bello: what about DLQ handling? any gotchas?

Rafael Mendes: DLQ gotchas:
- DLQ depth should be ~0. if it starts growing, do NOT spam replay.
- replay tool (guardrailed) lives in exporter image:
  `audit-exporter replay --tenant <id> --destination splunk --from-dlq --max-batches 50`
- always check downstream health first; replay while Splunk still 429’ing just makes things worse.
- replay will create duplicates; tell customer to dedup by `event_id`.

Sean Gallagher: +1. if DLQ > 1000 batches or growing steadily, page secondary and consider pausing exports to prevent runaway.

Rafael Mendes: When to involve Security (Marcus/Kevin):
- any sign of export sending to an unrecognized host / destination tampering (possible exfil)
- if logs contain unexpected sensitive fields (PII) or raw request payloads (should be redacted)
- if we suspect credentials exposure (Splunk HEC token / Datadog key showing up in logs, even partially)
- if customer asks for compliance language re: retention vs export responsibilities beyond the FAQ

Marcus Lin: +1. also loop us in if you’re asked to widen egress allowlist for a non-standard SIEM endpoint. we need threat-model review.

Rafael Mendes: Notes from today’s incident specifics:
- affected tenants: 2 (beta) using Splunk HEC; both had customer-side limit set lower than expected
- we paused Splunk for tenant A for ~22m, then resumed after customer confirmed HEC throttle settings updated
- no evidence of dropped events in exporter; checkpoints monotonic; DLQ remained at 0
- watch for recurrence around top of hour (their SIEM pipelines seemed to burst)

Aisha Bello: thanks. who is the POC if I need customer comms?

Rafael Mendes: customer comms: route through TAM/CSM; for the two tenants today it was Liam O'Rourke + Miguel Santos. if urgent/outage, use #customer-success + incident channel.

Logan Wright: also FYI we deployed a small config change earlier: reduced default Splunk batch payload size (helps avoid 413/429 cascades). no rollback expected.

Sean Gallagher: alright. Aisha, page me if lag breaches 30m or if auto-pause triggers on more than 3 tenants.

Aisha Bello: ack. I’m on it. :saluting_face:

Rafael Mendes: handing off. I’ll stay available for ~30m if anything flares.

title: #support: 1739478025
preview:
Dev Patel: hey team — as we prep for checklist rollout, can we align on support macros + triage questions keyed off checklist step state?

Dev Patel: goal: faster first response for the common onboarding issues (401/403, streaming disconnects, 429 surprises, “usage dashboard not updating”). if we can map these to step states, we can ask the right Qs immediately.

Priya Shah: +1. right now we ask a bunch of generic stuff and it’s slow. if Console shows “API key created” but “first successful request” not done, we should go straight to auth/env var troubleshooting.

Jasmine Liu: also support needs a clear “what does each step mean” in plain language. some customers will say “i did it” but event never fired.

Dev Patel: sharing a draft macro skeleton. pls critique.

Dev Patel: 
Macro (draft): “Onboarding checklist — triage”
1) Can you share your Redwood org + project name (or project id) and region?
2) Are you using Redwood SDK or OpenAI-compat?
3) What step is the checklist stuck on?
4) Please paste:
   - request URL (redact key)
   - HTTP status + response body
   - request-id / trace-id (if available)
   - your env var names (NOT values)
5) If streaming: what client/runtime (node/python/go) + are you behind a proxy?

Priya Shah: looks good. add a callout for “do not paste API keys”. we still get that.

SupportOps Bot: Reminder: Do not request or collect secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords). Use last-4 / key id where applicable.

Dev Patel: yup, will add.

Priya Shah: can we add checklist-specific branching? like if step shows “First 2xx request” complete but “Streaming established” incomplete, we ask different stuff.

Dev Patel: yep. proposing we publish 1 macro per high-frequency scenario. rough mapping:
- Step: “Create API key” incomplete → guide to Console key creation + permissions
- Step: “Set env + make first request” incomplete → 401/403 + base URL + wrong env var
- Step: “Streaming” incomplete → SSE disconnects, timeouts, proxy buffering
- Step: “Retries/timeouts” incomplete → prod defaults guidance + recommended settings
- Step: “Quotas/budgets” incomplete → 429 after launch + how to view quotas
- Step: “Usage dashboard” incomplete → explain usage lag + where to verify usage

Jasmine Liu: + add “Model pinned / fallback configured” (first prod launch). those are common enterprise questions.

Dev Patel: good call. though I think MVP checklist includes at least model pinning + fallback policy basics.

Kenji Nakamura: from UX side, step names are pretty short in Console. we can mirror the exact labels so support can quote them.

Dev Patel: can you drop the exact step labels as they’ll appear? don’t want drift.

Kenji Nakamura: current copy (subject to final):
- Create an API key
- Make your first request
- Try streaming
- Set timeouts + retries
- Review usage + costs
- Configure quotas / budgets
- Pin a model version
- Set a fallback policy
- Add basic observability (request IDs / logs)

Dev Patel: perfect.

Priya Shah: main pain: sometimes checklist shows wrong state / regresses. if we can’t fix instantly, support needs a way to override a step to unblock customer comms.

Dev Patel: yes — this is the admin override request. we need ability to (a) mark complete (b) reset (c) add note/audit trail. otherwise we end up telling customer “just ignore Console” which is… bad.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve heard this from dogfood. we can add a Support-only override panel, but permissions + audit logging need to be solid.

Chloe Martin: backend side, we can expose an admin endpoint in progress service: `POST /admin/tenants/{tenant_id}/checklist/steps/{step_id}:override` with action + reason. must be restricted to support role + logged.

Logan Wright: +1 but please don’t use override as a fix for event pipeline issues. we should make it clear: override is for customer comms / unblocking, not “hide broken telemetry”. maybe require a reason code.

Priya Shah: agreed. it’s mostly for cases where customer did the thing but event didn’t fire (e.g., first request succeeded via curl but from a different project? or user has multiple orgs).

Dev Patel: exactly. common patterns:
- customer used wrong project key first, then correct one; checklist looks “stuck” even though they’re now live
- usage dashboard lag makes them think nothing is happening; they spam test calls; checklist is ok but costs spike
- streaming step incomplete because client drops connection early; they still got tokens

Jasmine Liu: + account merges / multiple workspaces. checklist progress should be per project, but users think it’s per login.

Dylan Brooks: re override: we can do “mark as complete” and “reset”, but we need to ensure we don’t break the monotonic state model (out-of-order events issue).

Chloe Martin: override can be stored as a separate layer (support_override=true) and UI can show “Completed (Support override)” with a tooltip + link to audit entry.

Kenji Nakamura: that would be ideal. from UX, we should also display “last event seen” for each step (timestamp) in the support view.

Logan Wright: careful with timestamps shown to customers; for support-only view, ok. for customer view, keep it simple.

Dev Patel: can we add a support-only “debug” drawer? not customer visible, but lets us see:
- last onboarding event type + time
- request-id samples
- which SDK/user-agent tag

Chloe Martin: doable, but might be phase 2. MVP: endpoints + audit log + permissions.

Priya Shah: if MVP doesn’t include debug view, can we at least have a macro that asks the customer for the request-id and then we look it up internally?

Dev Patel: yes. updating macro templates:

Dev Patel: 
Macro: “Checklist stuck — Make your first request”
Questions:
1) Which SDK/compat layer?
2) Confirm base URL (Hosted API) + model name used
3) Confirm you set env var `REDWOOD_API_KEY` (or equivalent) and that key is for the same project shown in Console
4) Please send one request-id from a failing call and one from a successful call (if any)
5) Are you seeing 401 vs 403?
Notes for support:
- 401 usually wrong/missing key
- 403 can be project mismatch / key revoked / scope issue
- if step shows complete but customer still errors → likely different project/env

Priya Shah: add “are you using a key with leading/trailing whitespace” — surprisingly frequent.

Dev Patel: lol yes, adding.

Dev Patel:
Macro: “Checklist stuck — Try streaming”
Questions:
1) What language/runtime? Node version? Python requests vs httpx? Go net/http?
2) Are you behind Cloudflare / nginx / corporate proxy?
3) Do you see disconnect after N seconds or immediately?
4) Can you share client-side error + whether you receive partial tokens?
5) Are you using our recommended timeouts/retry preset?
Notes:
- proxies may buffer SSE; need `Cache-Control: no-transform` (if applicable)
- recommend client-side reconnect pattern + backoff

Jasmine Liu: for streaming, we should include a one-liner about “disconnect doesn’t mean request failed; check request-id and usage”. this will reduce panic.

Dev Patel: good.

Priya Shah:
Macro: “429 after launch — quotas/budgets”
Questions:
1) When did you go live? approx QPS + token/sec expected?
2) Which model + context lengths?
3) Are you seeing 429 from rate limit vs quota exceeded? (paste response JSON)
4) Did you configure budgets/quotas in Console?
Notes:
- explain backoff; don’t retry immediately in a tight loop
- if needs higher quota, route to account team / standard process

Dev Patel: yes.

Dylan Brooks: on admin override request — can you file an internal support ticket that describes exact workflow + permission expectations? we’ll link it to ENG work.

Dev Patel: already have INT-3307 open (request support admin override). will add these requirements:
- role-based access (support + CS only)
- audit log includes: who, when, step, action, reason, ticket link
- visible badge in Console: “Support override”
- ability to reset to “auto” (remove override)

Logan Wright: +1 to including ticket link. we can validate override usage volume.

Chloe Martin: for schema, reason codes could be like `CUSTOMER_CONFIRMED`, `TELEMETRY_DELAY`, `EVENT_MISSING`, `MIGRATION/LEGACY`, `OTHER`.

Priya Shah: love it. also please give us a way to “undo” if we mistakenly mark complete.

Chloe Martin: reset supported.

Kenji Nakamura: note: the UI copy “Support override” should probably be “Completed by Support” or similar. “override” sounds scary.

Dev Patel: agree — customer-facing phrasing should be gentle. internally we can still call it override.

Jasmine Liu: what’s the timeline? support enablement needs to know if we’ll have this before cohort rollout.

Dylan Brooks: we can likely ship admin endpoints quickly, UI support view might lag. if needed, support can hit endpoint via an internal tool (or we provide a simple console script) initially.

Dev Patel: ok. for rollout readiness, we’ll:
- finalize macros in Zendesk
- publish a 1-pager: “How to interpret checklist states”
- document escalation path: if checklist wrong → Telemetry (Logan) vs Console (Dylan/Chloe)

Priya Shah: can we also include “expected lag”? like usage dashboard can be up to X minutes behind, so support doesn’t treat it as incident.

Logan Wright: current SLA for usage aggregation is ~5-10 min typical, worst-case 30 min during backfills. we can put that in the macro.

Dev Patel: awesome. I’ll draft the 1-pager + update macros today. Dylan/Chloe pls keep us posted on admin override ETA.

Dylan Brooks: will do. :thumbsup:

Chloe Martin: :thumbsup: will add the requirements to ENG-5852 and loop you when we have staging.

Jasmine Liu: thanks all. will also coordinate with CS leads so they use the same macro language.

Priya Shah: +1. this will cut our onboarding tickets a lot if we’re consistent.
text:
Dev Patel: hey team — as we prep for checklist rollout, can we align on support macros + triage questions keyed off checklist step state?

Dev Patel: goal: faster first response for the common onboarding issues (401/403, streaming disconnects, 429 surprises, “usage dashboard not updating”). if we can map these to step states, we can ask the right Qs immediately.

Priya Shah: +1. right now we ask a bunch of generic stuff and it’s slow. if Console shows “API key created” but “first successful request” not done, we should go straight to auth/env var troubleshooting.

Jasmine Liu: also support needs a clear “what does each step mean” in plain language. some customers will say “i did it” but event never fired.

Dev Patel: sharing a draft macro skeleton. pls critique.

Dev Patel: 
Macro (draft): “Onboarding checklist — triage”
1) Can you share your Redwood org + project name (or project id) and region?
2) Are you using Redwood SDK or OpenAI-compat?
3) What step is the checklist stuck on?
4) Please paste:
   - request URL (redact key)
   - HTTP status + response body
   - request-id / trace-id (if available)
   - your env var names (NOT values)
5) If streaming: what client/runtime (node/python/go) + are you behind a proxy?

Priya Shah: looks good. add a callout for “do not paste API keys”. we still get that.

SupportOps Bot: Reminder: Do not request or collect secrets (API keys, tokens, passwords). Use last-4 / key id where applicable.

Dev Patel: yup, will add.

Priya Shah: can we add checklist-specific branching? like if step shows “First 2xx request” complete but “Streaming established” incomplete, we ask different stuff.

Dev Patel: yep. proposing we publish 1 macro per high-frequency scenario. rough mapping:
- Step: “Create API key” incomplete → guide to Console key creation + permissions
- Step: “Set env + make first request” incomplete → 401/403 + base URL + wrong env var
- Step: “Streaming” incomplete → SSE disconnects, timeouts, proxy buffering
- Step: “Retries/timeouts” incomplete → prod defaults guidance + recommended settings
- Step: “Quotas/budgets” incomplete → 429 after launch + how to view quotas
- Step: “Usage dashboard” incomplete → explain usage lag + where to verify usage

Jasmine Liu: + add “Model pinned / fallback configured” (first prod launch). those are common enterprise questions.

Dev Patel: good call. though I think MVP checklist includes at least model pinning + fallback policy basics.

Kenji Nakamura: from UX side, step names are pretty short in Console. we can mirror the exact labels so support can quote them.

Dev Patel: can you drop the exact step labels as they’ll appear? don’t want drift.

Kenji Nakamura: current copy (subject to final):
- Create an API key
- Make your first request
- Try streaming
- Set timeouts + retries
- Review usage + costs
- Configure quotas / budgets
- Pin a model version
- Set a fallback policy
- Add basic observability (request IDs / logs)

Dev Patel: perfect.

Priya Shah: main pain: sometimes checklist shows wrong state / regresses. if we can’t fix instantly, support needs a way to override a step to unblock customer comms.

Dev Patel: yes — this is the admin override request. we need ability to (a) mark complete (b) reset (c) add note/audit trail. otherwise we end up telling customer “just ignore Console” which is… bad.

Dylan Brooks: we’ve heard this from dogfood. we can add a Support-only override panel, but permissions + audit logging need to be solid.

Chloe Martin: backend side, we can expose an admin endpoint in progress service: `POST /admin/tenants/{tenant_id}/checklist/steps/{step_id}:override` with action + reason. must be restricted to support role + logged.

Logan Wright: +1 but please don’t use override as a fix for event pipeline issues. we should make it clear: override is for customer comms / unblocking, not “hide broken telemetry”. maybe require a reason code.

Priya Shah: agreed. it’s mostly for cases where customer did the thing but event didn’t fire (e.g., first request succeeded via curl but from a different project? or user has multiple orgs).

Dev Patel: exactly. common patterns:
- customer used wrong project key first, then correct one; checklist looks “stuck” even though they’re now live
- usage dashboard lag makes them think nothing is happening; they spam test calls; checklist is ok but costs spike
- streaming step incomplete because client drops connection early; they still got tokens

Jasmine Liu: + account merges / multiple workspaces. checklist progress should be per project, but users think it’s per login.

Dylan Brooks: re override: we can do “mark as complete” and “reset”, but we need to ensure we don’t break the monotonic state model (out-of-order events issue).

Chloe Martin: override can be stored as a separate layer (support_override=true) and UI can show “Completed (Support override)” with a tooltip + link to audit entry.

Kenji Nakamura: that would be ideal. from UX, we should also display “last event seen” for each step (timestamp) in the support view.

Logan Wright: careful with timestamps shown to customers; for support-only view, ok. for customer view, keep it simple.

Dev Patel: can we add a support-only “debug” drawer? not customer visible, but lets us see:
- last onboarding event type + time
- request-id samples
- which SDK/user-agent tag

Chloe Martin: doable, but might be phase 2. MVP: endpoints + audit log + permissions.

Priya Shah: if MVP doesn’t include debug view, can we at least have a macro that asks the customer for the request-id and then we look it up internally?
